approach: Filter rows using WHERE with OR condition on area and population thresholds.
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: Scans each row once to check the conditions.
space_complexity_explanation: No additional space beyond the result set.
key_insight: Use OR to combine two independent filtering conditions in a single WHERE clause.

title: Fizz Buzz
hints: Iterate from 1 to n and check divisibility conditions.
Check divisibility by 15 first (both 3 and 5), then by 3, then by 5.
Use the modulo operator to check divisibility.
approach: Iterate 1 to n. For each number check divisibility by 15, 3, and 5 in order, appending the appropriate string.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through each number from 1 to n exactly once.
space_complexity_explanation: The result array stores n strings.
key_insight: Check divisibility by 15 before checking 3 or 5 individually to handle the combined case.

title: Count Good Numbers
hints: Even indices (0-indexed) can have 5 choices (0,2,4,6,8) and odd indices can have 4 choices (2,3,5,7).
Count how many even and odd positions there are for a string of length n.
Use modular exponentiation to efficiently compute large powers modulo 10^9+7.
approach: For a string of length n, even positions have 5 choices and odd positions have 4 choices. Compute 5^(ceil(n/2)) * 4^(floor(n/2)) mod 10^9+7 using fast exponentiation.
time_complexity_explanation: Modular exponentiation uses binary exponentiation which runs in O(log n).
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used.
key_insight: The total count is 5^(even positions) * 4^(odd positions), computed efficiently with modular exponentiation.

title: Combine Two Tables
hints: You need information from both Person and Address tables.
Not every person has an address, so you need a LEFT JOIN to keep all persons.
Join on the personId column that exists in both tables.
approach: Use a LEFT JOIN from Person to Address on personId to include all persons even if they have no address.
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The join processes each row from both tables.
space_complexity_explanation: No additional space beyond the result set.
key_insight: LEFT JOIN ensures every person appears in the result even without a matching address.

title: Permutations II
hints: Sort the array first so duplicates are adjacent.
Use a visited array and skip a number if it's the same as the previous one and the previous one wasn't used in this branch.
Backtrack by unmarking visited and removing from the current path.
approach: Sort the array, then use backtracking with a visited array. Skip duplicates by checking if the current element equals the previous and the previous wasn't visited.
time_complexity_explanation: In the worst case we generate n! permutations, each taking O(n) to copy.
space_complexity_explanation: Recursion stack depth is O(n), plus the used array of size n.
key_insight: Sort first, then skip duplicate elements at the same recursion level to avoid generating duplicate permutations.

title: Longest Substring with At Most K Distinct Characters
hints: Use a sliding window with two pointers and a hash map to track character counts.
Expand the right pointer and add characters. When distinct characters exceed k, shrink from the left.
Track the maximum window size throughout the process.
approach: Use a sliding window with a hash map counting character frequencies. Expand right, and when distinct count exceeds k, shrink from left until valid again.
time_complexity_explanation: Each character is added and removed from the window at most once.
space_complexity_explanation: The hash map stores at most k+1 distinct characters.
key_insight: A sliding window with a hash map efficiently tracks the number of distinct characters in the current substring.

title: Online Stock Span
hints: Think about how many consecutive previous days had a price <= today's price.
Use a stack that stores (price, span) pairs.
When a new price comes in, pop all stack entries with price <= current price and accumulate their spans.
approach: Maintain a monotonic decreasing stack of (price, span) pairs. For each new price, pop entries with smaller or equal prices, summing their spans into the current span.
time_complexity_explanation: Each element is pushed and popped from the stack at most once across all calls.
space_complexity_explanation: The stack can hold up to n elements in the worst case.
key_insight: A monotonic decreasing stack lets you accumulate spans by absorbing smaller elements as they are popped.

title: Check if Array Is Sorted and Rotated
hints: In a sorted and rotated array, there is at most one point where the order decreases.
Count the number of places where nums[i] > nums[i+1] (including wrap-around).
If the count is 0 or 1, the array is sorted and rotated.
approach: Count the number of inversions (nums[i] > nums[(i+1) % n]). A valid sorted-and-rotated array has at most one inversion.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the array of length n.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a counter variable is used.
key_insight: A sorted-and-rotated array has at most one 'break point' where the value decreases, including the wrap-around from last to first element.

title: Find All Duplicates in an Array
hints: Elements are in range [1, n] where n is the array length. Use the array indices as a hash.
For each number, negate the value at the index corresponding to that number.
If the value at that index is already negative, the number is a duplicate.
approach: Use the array itself as a hash map. For each value, mark the element at that index as negative. If it's already negative, the value is a duplicate.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the array.
space_complexity_explanation: No extra space used apart from the result array; modifications are in-place.
key_insight: Since values are in [1, n], use the sign of elements at corresponding indices to detect duplicates without extra space.

title: Add Strings
hints: Simulate addition digit by digit from right to left, just like manual addition.
Use two pointers starting at the end of each string and track the carry.
Don't forget to handle the remaining carry after processing all digits.
approach: Simulate grade-school addition from right to left using two pointers and a carry variable. Build the result string in reverse.
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def addStrings(self, num1: str, num2: str) -> str:
        i, j = len(num1) - 1, len(num2) - 1
        carry = 0
        result = []
        while i >= 0 or j >= 0 or carry:
            x = int(num1[i]) if i >= 0 else 0
            y = int(num2[j]) if j >= 0 else 0
            total = x + y + carry
            result.append(str(total % 10))
            carry = total // 10
            i -= 1
            j -= 1
        return ''.join(reversed(result))
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through the longer of the two strings.
space_complexity_explanation: The result string has length proportional to the longer input.
key_insight: Process digits from right to left with a carry, just like manual addition on paper.

title: Maximal Rectangle
hints: Build a histogram of heights for each row, where height[j] is the number of consecutive 1s above (including current row).
For each row's histogram, apply the Largest Rectangle in Histogram algorithm.
Use a monotonic stack to find the largest rectangle in each histogram in O(n) time.
approach: For each row, build a histogram of heights (consecutive 1s in each column). Then apply the largest rectangle in histogram algorithm using a monotonic stack for each row.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of the m rows, we process n columns with the stack algorithm in O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: The heights array and stack each use O(n) space.
key_insight: Reduce the 2D problem to repeated 1D 'largest rectangle in histogram' problems by building column histograms row by row.

title: Minimum Edge Reversals So Every Node Is Reachable
hints: The graph is a tree with n-1 edges. First compute the answer for one root using DFS.
When traversing an edge in its original direction, cost is 0; in reverse direction, cost is 1.
Use rerooting technique: when moving the root from parent to child, adjust the cost based on the edge direction.
approach: Use rerooting DP on a tree. First DFS from node 0 to compute reversals needed. Then re-root: moving root from u to child v, if edge is u->v, add 1 reversal (now need to reverse it); if v->u, subtract 1 (no longer need to reverse it).
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def minEdgeReversals(self, n: int, edges: list[list[int]]) -> list[int]:
        adj = [[] for _ in range(n)]
        for u, v in edges:
            adj[u].append((v, 0))
            adj[v].append((u, 1))
        ans = [0] * n
        # DFS from 0 to compute ans[0]
        stack = [(0, -1)]
        order = []
        parent = [-1] * n
        cost_to_parent = [0] * n
        while stack:
            node, par = stack.pop()
            order.append(node)
            parent[node] = par
            for nei, cost in adj[node]:
                if nei != par:
                    ans[0] += cost
                    cost_to_parent[nei] = cost
                    stack.append((nei, node))
        # Rerooting
        for node in order:
            if parent[node] == -1:
                continue
            # cost_to_parent[node]: cost to go from parent to node
            # if cost is 1, edge is child->parent (reversed), moving root saves 1
            # if cost is 0, edge is parent->child (forward), moving root costs 1
            ans[node] = ans[parent[node]] + (1 if cost_to_parent[node] == 0 else -1)
        return ans
time_complexity_explanation: Two passes over the tree: one DFS and one rerooting pass, each O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: Adjacency list, parent, cost arrays, and order array all use O(n) space.
key_insight: Use rerooting DP: compute the answer for one root, then propagate to all others by adjusting the cost when shifting the root along each edge.

title: Minimum Operations to Reduce an Integer to 0
hints: You can add or subtract powers of 2. Think about the binary representation.
Consecutive 1-bits can be handled more efficiently by adding a power of 2 to turn them into a single 1-bit at a higher position.
A single 1-bit costs 1 operation. A streak of consecutive 1-bits costs 2 operations (add to create carry, then subtract).
approach: Process the binary representation. For each group of consecutive 1-bits: a single 1 costs 1 operation, a streak of 2+ consecutive 1s costs 2 operations (add then subtract). Handle carry propagation.
time_complexity_explanation: We process each bit of n, and n has O(log n) bits. Adding 1 may increase length by 1 but total operations remain logarithmic.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used.
key_insight: When consecutive 1-bits appear, it's cheaper to add a power of 2 to eliminate the streak than to subtract each bit individually.

title: Simplify Path
hints: Split the path by '/' and process each component.
Use a stack: push valid directory names, pop for '..', ignore '.' and empty strings.
Join the stack with '/' and prepend '/' for the final canonical path.
approach: Split by '/', use a stack to track directories. Push names, pop for '..', skip '.' and empty. Join with '/'.
time_complexity_explanation: We process each character of the path once during split and stack operations.
space_complexity_explanation: The stack and split parts can hold up to O(n) elements.
key_insight: Split by '/' and use a stack to handle directory navigation — push for names, pop for '..'.

title: Subarray Sums Divisible by K
hints: A subarray sum is divisible by k if prefix_sum[j] % k == prefix_sum[i] % k.
Use a hash map to count prefix sums with the same remainder modulo k.
Handle negative remainders by adding k before taking modulo.
approach: Track prefix sum remainders mod k in a hash map. For each new prefix sum, the count of previous prefix sums with the same remainder gives the number of valid subarrays ending here.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the array with O(1) hash map operations per element.
space_complexity_explanation: The hash map stores at most k different remainders.
key_insight: Two prefix sums with the same remainder mod k define a subarray whose sum is divisible by k.

title: Minimum Value to Get Positive Step by Step Sum
hints: Compute the running prefix sum as you iterate through the array.
Track the minimum prefix sum encountered.
The answer is 1 - min_prefix_sum, ensuring the step-by-step sum is always >= 1.
approach: Find the minimum prefix sum. The starting value must be large enough that start + min_prefix_sum >= 1, so return max(1, 1 - min_prefix_sum).
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the array to compute prefix sums.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two variables are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: The minimum starting value equals 1 minus the minimum prefix sum, ensuring the running total never drops below 1.

title: Running Sum of 1d Array
hints: The running sum at index i is the sum of all elements from index 0 to i.
You can compute it in-place by adding the previous element to the current one.
Start from index 1 and set nums[i] += nums[i-1].
approach: Iterate from index 1 to n-1, accumulating the sum in-place by adding the previous element to the current one.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the array.
space_complexity_explanation: The computation is done in-place with no extra space.
key_insight: Each running sum element is just the previous running sum plus the current element, computable in-place.

title: Rotate List
hints: First find the length of the list and reduce k by taking k % length.
Make the list circular by connecting the tail to the head.
Find the new tail at position (length - k - 1) from the start, break the circle there.
approach: Find the length, reduce k = k % length. Connect tail to head forming a cycle. Move (length - k) steps from head to find the new tail, break the cycle.
time_complexity_explanation: We traverse the list twice at most: once to find the length and once to find the split point.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of pointers are used.
key_insight: Form a circular list and break it at the right position — the new head is at position (length - k) from the original head.

title: Largest Number
hints: Compare two numbers by checking which concatenation order produces a larger result.
For numbers a and b, compare str(a)+str(b) vs str(b)+str(a).
Handle the edge case where the result is all zeros (return '0').
approach: Convert numbers to strings and sort with a custom comparator: for a and b, compare a+b vs b+a. Concatenate the sorted result.
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def largestNumber(self, nums: list[int]) -> str:
        from functools import cmp_to_key
        s = list(map(str, nums))
        s.sort(key=cmp_to_key(lambda a, b: (1 if a + b < b + a else -1 if a + b > b + a else 0)))
        result = ''.join(s)
        return '0' if result[0] == '0' else result
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting n strings with comparisons that take O(k) time each, where k is the average string length.
space_complexity_explanation: Storage for string representations of all numbers.
key_insight: Custom sort by comparing concatenation orders (a+b vs b+a) ensures the optimal arrangement for the largest number.

title: Flood Fill
hints: Start from the given pixel and explore all 4-directionally connected pixels with the same original color.
Use DFS or BFS to visit connected pixels and change their color.
If the starting pixel already has the new color, return immediately to avoid infinite loops.
approach: Use DFS from the starting pixel. Change each visited pixel to the new color and recurse on 4-directional neighbors with the original color. Skip if new color equals original.
time_complexity_explanation: In the worst case, every pixel in the image is visited once.
space_complexity_explanation: Recursion stack can go as deep as the number of pixels in the worst case.
key_insight: Early return when original color equals new color prevents infinite recursion and handles the trivial case.

title: Divide Two Integers
hints: Think about how you can use bit shifting to efficiently subtract multiples of the divisor.
Double the divisor repeatedly (left shift) until it exceeds the dividend, then subtract and repeat.
Handle edge cases: overflow when dividing INT_MIN by -1, and sign handling.
approach: Use bit shifting to perform division without multiply/divide operators. Repeatedly double the divisor via left shifts until it exceeds the dividend, subtract the largest fitting multiple, and accumulate the quotient. Handle sign separately and clamp to 32-bit integer range.
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def divide(self, dividend: int, divisor: int) -> int:
        INT_MAX = 2**31 - 1
        INT_MIN = -2**31
        if dividend == INT_MIN and divisor == -1:
            return INT_MAX
        sign = -1 if (dividend < 0) ^ (divisor < 0) else 1
        a, b = abs(dividend), abs(divisor)
        result = 0
        while a >= b:
            temp, multiple = b, 1
            while a >= (temp << 1):
                temp <<= 1
                multiple <<= 1
            a -= temp
            result += multiple
        return sign * result
time_complexity_explanation: The outer loop runs O(log n) times as we subtract exponentially growing multiples, and each inner doubling loop runs O(log n) times.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: Bit shifting the divisor lets you subtract exponentially larger multiples, turning repeated subtraction from O(n) into O(log^2 n).

title: Reverse Bits
hints: Process one bit at a time from the input and build the result from the opposite end.
Use AND with 1 to extract the last bit, then shift the result left and the input right.
Always process exactly 32 bits, even if the leading bits are zero.
approach: Iterate 32 times. In each iteration, left-shift the result by 1, extract the last bit of n using AND 1, OR it into the result, then right-shift n by 1.
time_complexity_explanation: We always iterate exactly 32 times regardless of the input value.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a single integer variable is used to store the result.
key_insight: Extract bits from the right of the input and place them from the left of the output by shifting in opposite directions.

title: Integer to English Words
hints: Break the number into groups of three digits and handle each group with a helper function.
Each group of three maps to a scale word: Thousand, Million, Billion.
Handle the teens (11-19) as special cases since they don't follow the regular ones/tens pattern.
approach: Process the number in chunks of 1000. For each chunk, convert the three-digit number to words using lookup arrays for ones, teens, and tens. Append the appropriate scale word (Thousand, Million, Billion) for each chunk's position.
time_complexity_explanation: The input is bounded by 2^31 - 1, so we process at most 4 groups of 3 digits each. The work is constant.
space_complexity_explanation: The lookup arrays are fixed size and the result string length is bounded by a constant for any valid input.
key_insight: Break the number into groups of three digits and recursively convert each group, appending scale words (Thousand, Million, Billion) based on position.

title: Minimum Difficulty of a Job Schedule
hints: Think of dp[i][d] as the minimum difficulty to schedule the first i jobs in d days.
On the last day, you must do at least one job; iterate over all possible starting points for the last day.
The difficulty of a day is the max job difficulty on that day — track it as you expand the range.
approach: Use DP where dp[i][d] represents the minimum difficulty to schedule jobs 0..i-1 over d days. For each state, try all possible partitions for the last day, tracking the max difficulty as you extend the last day's range backward. Return -1 if there are fewer jobs than days.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of the n*d states, we iterate up to n jobs backward to find the last day's partition, giving O(n^2 * d).
space_complexity_explanation: The DP table has (n+1) * (d+1) entries.
key_insight: Partition the job array into exactly d contiguous subarrays and minimize the sum of each subarray's maximum — classic interval DP.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II
hints: You can make as many transactions as you want — think about what the optimal strategy looks like.
Every time the price goes up from one day to the next, you can capture that profit.
The sum of all positive consecutive differences equals the maximum total profit.
approach: Greedily collect every upward price movement. Iterate through the array and whenever the price on day i is greater than day i-1, add the difference to the total profit. This captures all possible gains.
time_complexity_explanation: We make a single pass through the prices array of length n.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a single variable is used to track the running profit.
key_insight: With unlimited transactions, capturing every consecutive price increase is equivalent to finding the optimal buy/sell points.

title: Monthly Transactions I
hints: Group transactions by both month and country.
Use DATE_FORMAT or SUBSTRING to extract the year-month from the transaction date.
Use conditional aggregation (SUM with CASE/IF) to compute approved counts and amounts.
approach: Group by month (extracted via DATE_FORMAT) and country. Use COUNT for total transactions, SUM with IF/CASE for approved counts and amounts.
solution_python: SELECT DATE_FORMAT(trans_date, '%Y-%m') AS month, country, COUNT(*) AS trans_count, SUM(IF(state = 'approved', 1, 0)) AS approved_count, SUM(amount) AS trans_total_amount, SUM(IF(state = 'approved', amount, 0)) AS approved_total_amount FROM Transactions GROUP BY month, country;
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The query scans all n rows in the Transactions table once and groups them.
space_complexity_explanation: The result set size is proportional to the number of distinct (month, country) groups.
key_insight: Conditional aggregation with SUM(IF(...)) lets you compute filtered totals within a single GROUP BY query.

title: Minimum Absolute Difference
hints: The minimum absolute difference must occur between adjacent elements in a sorted array.
Sort the array first, then make one pass to find the minimum difference.
Make a second pass (or combine into one) to collect all pairs with that minimum difference.
approach: Sort the array. The minimum absolute difference can only occur between consecutive elements. Find this minimum in one pass, then collect all consecutive pairs that achieve it.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting dominates at O(n log n). The subsequent linear passes are O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: The output list can contain up to O(n) pairs in the worst case.
key_insight: After sorting, the minimum absolute difference only exists between adjacent elements, reducing the problem from O(n^2) comparisons to O(n).

title: Customer Who Visited but Did Not Make Any Transactions
hints: Use a LEFT JOIN from Visits to Transactions to find visits without matching transactions.
Filter for rows where the transaction side is NULL after the join.
Group by customer_id and count to get the number of no-transaction visits.
approach: LEFT JOIN Visits with Transactions on visit_id. Filter where transaction_id IS NULL to isolate visits with no purchase. Group by customer_id and count the visits.
solution_python: SELECT v.customer_id, COUNT(*) AS count_no_trans FROM Visits v LEFT JOIN Transactions t ON v.visit_id = t.visit_id WHERE t.transaction_id IS NULL GROUP BY v.customer_id;
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The join scans both tables (n visits, m transactions) once with proper indexing.
space_complexity_explanation: The result contains at most one row per customer who visited without transacting.
key_insight: A LEFT JOIN with a NULL check on the right table's key is the standard pattern for finding unmatched rows.

title: Perfect Number
hints: A perfect number equals the sum of its proper divisors (excluding itself).
You only need to check divisors up to sqrt(num), adding both the divisor and its complement.
Start the sum at 1 (since 1 is always a divisor) and be careful not to double-count sqrt(num).
approach: Iterate from 2 to sqrt(num). For each divisor i, add both i and num/i to the sum. Start with sum=1. Check if the total equals num. Handle edge case where num <= 1.
time_complexity_explanation: We only iterate up to the square root of num to find all divisor pairs.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used.
key_insight: Divisors come in pairs (i, num/i), so checking up to sqrt(num) finds all divisors in O(sqrt(n)) time.

title: Valid Palindrome II
hints: Use two pointers from both ends moving inward to check for palindrome.
When you find a mismatch, you have one chance — try skipping either the left or right character.
Check if either of the two resulting substrings is a palindrome.
approach: Use two pointers from both ends. When a mismatch is found, check if skipping the left character or the right character yields a palindrome substring. If either does, return true.
time_complexity_explanation: We traverse the string at most twice — once in the main loop and once in the helper check.
space_complexity_explanation: Only pointer variables are used; no extra data structures.
key_insight: At the first mismatch you have exactly two choices (skip left or skip right) — try both and see if either creates a palindrome.

title: Delete Duplicate Emails
hints: You need to keep the row with the smallest id for each duplicate email.
Use a self-join to find rows that share the same email but have a larger id.
DELETE the rows from the table where a matching row with a smaller id exists.
approach: Self-join the Person table on email. Delete rows where the id is greater than another row's id with the same email, keeping only the row with the smallest id per email.
solution_python: DELETE p1 FROM Person p1 INNER JOIN Person p2 WHERE p1.email = p2.email AND p1.id > p2.id;
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The self-join compares each pair of rows with the same email, which is O(n^2) in the worst case without indexing.
space_complexity_explanation: The deletion is performed in-place with no additional storage.
key_insight: A self-join on email with an id inequality lets you identify and delete all duplicate rows while preserving the one with the smallest id.

title: Maximum Average Subarray I
hints: Use a sliding window of size k to avoid recalculating the sum from scratch each time.
Compute the sum of the first k elements, then slide by adding the next element and removing the first.
Track the maximum sum and divide by k at the end to get the maximum average.
approach: Compute the sum of the first k elements. Slide the window one element at a time, updating the sum by adding the incoming element and subtracting the outgoing one. Track the maximum sum and return max_sum / k.
time_complexity_explanation: We traverse the array once with the sliding window.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a few variables are used to track the window sum and maximum.
key_insight: A fixed-size sliding window lets you update the subarray sum in O(1) per step instead of recomputing from scratch.

title: Permutation Sequence
hints: There are (n-1)! permutations starting with each digit — use this to determine the first digit directly.
Convert k to 0-indexed, then repeatedly divide by the factorial to pick each digit.
Maintain a list of available digits and remove each one as you pick it.
approach: Use the factorial number system. Convert k to 0-indexed. For each position, determine which digit goes there by dividing k by (n-1)!, pick that digit from the remaining available digits, then update k to the remainder.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate n times, and each digit removal from the list costs O(n), giving O(n^2) total.
space_complexity_explanation: We store the list of available digits and the result string, both of size n.
key_insight: The factorial number system lets you directly compute the k-th permutation digit by digit without generating all permutations.

title: Classes With at Least 5 Students
hints: Group the rows by class to count students in each class.
Use HAVING to filter groups after aggregation.
The HAVING clause filters on aggregate results, unlike WHERE which filters individual rows.
approach: Group the Courses table by class, count the students in each group, and use HAVING to return only classes with 5 or more students.
solution_python: SELECT class FROM Courses GROUP BY class HAVING COUNT(student) >= 5;
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The query scans all n rows once for grouping and counting.
space_complexity_explanation: Space is proportional to the number of distinct classes for the grouping operation.
key_insight: GROUP BY with HAVING is the standard pattern for filtering based on aggregate conditions like counts or sums.

title: Design HashMap
hints: Use an array of buckets where each bucket is a linked list or dynamic array to handle collisions.
Choose a prime number as the bucket count to reduce collision clustering.
For each operation, hash the key to find the bucket, then scan the bucket for the key.
approach: Implement a hash map using an array of buckets (lists) with separate chaining. Hash the key modulo a prime bucket count to determine the bucket. For put, update if key exists or append. For get and remove, scan the bucket linearly.
time_complexity_explanation: Each operation takes O(n/k) average time where n is the number of stored keys and k is the bucket count, due to scanning a single bucket.
space_complexity_explanation: We store k buckets plus all n key-value pairs distributed across them.
key_insight: Separate chaining with a fixed array of buckets provides a simple hash map implementation where collisions are resolved by scanning a short linked list.

title: Managers with at Least 5 Direct Reports
hints: Each employee's managerId points to their manager's id in the same table.
Group employees by managerId and count to find managers with 5+ reports.
Join back to the Employee table to get the manager's name.
approach: Group employees by managerId, filter groups having at least 5 members using HAVING, then join back to the Employee table to retrieve the manager's name.
solution_python: SELECT e.name FROM Employee e JOIN (SELECT managerId FROM Employee GROUP BY managerId HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5) m ON e.id = m.managerId;
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The query scans the Employee table once for grouping and once for the join.
space_complexity_explanation: Intermediate grouping results are proportional to the number of distinct managers.
key_insight: Self-referencing foreign keys (managerId -> id) are queried by grouping on the foreign key and joining back for the referenced row's details.

title: Special Binary String
hints: A special binary string is like balanced parentheses — 1 is '(' and 0 is ')'.
Find all top-level special substrings (where the running count returns to 0), recurse inside each.
Sort the top-level special substrings in descending order to get the lexicographically largest result.
approach: Recursively find all top-level special substrings by tracking the balance of 1s and 0s. For each special substring, strip the outer characters (first '1' and last '0'), recurse on the inner part, then wrap back. Sort the top-level specials in descending order and concatenate.
time_complexity_explanation: Each character is processed in the recursion, and substring creation plus sorting at each level gives O(n^2) total work.
space_complexity_explanation: Recursive calls create substrings at each level; in the worst case the total substring storage is O(n^2).
key_insight: Special binary strings are isomorphic to balanced parentheses — sorting top-level balanced groups in descending order yields the lexicographically largest valid arrangement.

title: Is Graph Bipartite?
hints: Try to 2-color the graph — if you can, it's bipartite.
Use BFS or DFS: assign a color to a node, then assign the opposite color to all its neighbors.
If you ever find a neighbor with the same color as the current node, the graph is not bipartite.
approach: Use BFS/DFS to attempt a 2-coloring. Start from each uncolored node, assign it color 0, and assign the opposite color to its neighbors. If a neighbor already has the same color, return false. Handle disconnected components by iterating over all nodes.
time_complexity_explanation: BFS visits each vertex once and traverses each edge once across all components.
space_complexity_explanation: The color array and BFS queue together use O(V) space.
key_insight: A graph is bipartite if and only if it is 2-colorable — BFS/DFS coloring detects odd-length cycles that prevent bipartiteness.

title: Design Authentication Manager
hints: Use a hash map to store each token's expiration time.
A token is unexpired if its expiry time is strictly greater than the current time.
For renew, only update if the token exists and hasn't expired; for countUnexpiredTokens, count tokens with expiry > currentTime.
approach: Maintain a dictionary mapping tokenId to its expiration time. Generate sets expiry to currentTime + timeToLive. Renew updates the expiry only if the token exists and hasn't expired. Count iterates the map and counts tokens with expiry > currentTime.
time_complexity_explanation: Generate and renew are O(1). countUnexpiredTokens scans all n tokens in the map.
space_complexity_explanation: The hash map stores up to n tokens, one entry per generated token.
key_insight: Store expiration timestamps in a hash map — renew only if the token hasn't expired, and count by filtering on the current time.

title: Final Prices With a Special Discount in a Shop
hints: For each item, you need the first subsequent item with a price less than or equal to it.
A monotonic stack efficiently finds the next smaller or equal element for all positions.
Push indices onto the stack; when the current price is <= the stack top's price, pop and apply the discount.
approach: Use a monotonic stack to find the next element less than or equal to each price. Iterate through prices, and for each price, pop all stack elements whose price is >= current price, applying the discount. Push the current index onto the stack.
time_complexity_explanation: Each index is pushed and popped from the stack at most once, giving O(n) total operations.
space_complexity_explanation: The stack and result array each use O(n) space.
key_insight: A monotonic stack finds the next smaller-or-equal element for every position in a single pass, turning a naive O(n^2) search into O(n).

title: Average Selling Price
hints: Join the Prices table with UnitsSold on product_id and date range.
Use SUM(price * units) / SUM(units) for weighted average.
Handle products with no sales using LEFT JOIN and IFNULL/COALESCE.
approach: Left join Prices with UnitsSold matching on product_id and date within the price period. Compute weighted average price as total revenue divided by total units, rounding to 2 decimals. Use IFNULL to handle products with no sales.
solution_python:
SELECT p.product_id, IFNULL(ROUND(SUM(p.price * u.units) / SUM(u.units), 2), 0) AS average_price
FROM Prices p
LEFT JOIN UnitsSold u
  ON p.product_id = u.product_id
  AND u.purchase_date BETWEEN p.start_date AND p.end_date
GROUP BY p.product_id;
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: Join between Prices (n rows) and UnitsSold (m rows) with grouping.
space_complexity_explanation: Output has at most n distinct products.
key_insight: Use a LEFT JOIN with a date range condition to match each sale to its valid price period, then compute the weighted average.

title: Number of Good Pairs
hints: For each number, count how many times it has appeared before.
Each previous occurrence can form a good pair with the current element.
Use a hash map to track counts as you iterate.
approach: Iterate through the array maintaining a count of each number seen so far. For each element, the number of good pairs it forms equals the count of that number already seen. Sum these up.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the array with O(1) hash map operations.
space_complexity_explanation: Hash map stores at most n distinct values.
key_insight: When you encounter a number that has appeared k times before, it forms k new good pairs with the current element.

title: Longest Substring with At Least K Repeating Characters
hints: Characters that appear fewer than k times cannot be part of any valid substring.
Split the string on characters with frequency less than k and recurse.
Alternatively, use sliding window by fixing the number of unique characters allowed.
approach: Use divide and conquer: count character frequencies, then find any character appearing fewer than k times. Split the string at those characters and recursively find the longest valid substring in each segment. Base case: if all characters appear at least k times, return the string length.
time_complexity_explanation: At most 26 levels of recursion (one per unique character), each scanning the string in O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: Recursion depth up to 26 and split substrings total to O(n).
key_insight: Any character appearing fewer than k times in the whole string cannot be part of a valid substring, so use it as a split point for divide and conquer.

title: Maximum Number of Non-overlapping Palindrome Substrings
hints: Precompute all palindromic substrings of length >= k.
Use DP where dp[i] = max non-overlapping palindromes in s[0..i-1].
Greedily pick the shortest valid palindrome ending at each position to leave room for more.
approach: Use DP with Manacher-like palindrome checking. For each position i, either skip it (dp[i] = dp[i-1]) or end a palindrome here. For each valid palindrome ending at i with length >= k, update dp[i+1] = max(dp[i+1], dp[start] + 1). Optimize by only considering the shortest palindrome of length k or k+1 ending at each position.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of n positions, check at most 2 palindromes of length k or k+1, each taking O(k) to verify.
space_complexity_explanation: DP array of size n+1.
key_insight: To maximize the count of non-overlapping palindromes, greedily pick the shortest valid palindrome (length k or k+1) ending at each position, since shorter palindromes leave more room for others.

title: Shifting Letters
hints: shifts[i] applies to all characters from index 0 to i.
Compute the suffix sum of shifts to get the total shift for each character.
Apply modulo 26 to handle wrap-around.
approach: Compute suffix sums of the shifts array. Each character at index i gets shifted by the sum of shifts[i..n-1]. Apply this shift modulo 26 to each character.
time_complexity_explanation: Single reverse pass through the string and shifts array.
space_complexity_explanation: Result array of size n for the output string.
key_insight: Each shift[i] affects characters 0 through i, so processing right to left and accumulating a running total gives the total shift for each position.

title: Count Servers that Communicate
hints: Count the number of servers in each row and each column.
A server communicates if its row or column has more than one server.
Two passes: one to count, one to check.
approach: First pass: count servers in each row and column. Second pass: a server can communicate if the count of servers in its row or column is greater than 1.
time_complexity_explanation: Two passes through the entire grid.
space_complexity_explanation: Arrays to store row and column counts.
key_insight: A server communicates if and only if there is at least one other server in the same row or same column.

title: Twisted Mirror Path Count
hints: Simulate the light beam path through the grid of mirrors.
Track the direction changes when hitting \\ and / mirrors.
Count unique cells visited or detect cycles to determine path length.
approach: Simulate light paths from each entry point on the grid border. Track direction (up/down/left/right) and change it according to mirror type at each cell. Count paths that exit the grid or detect cycles using visited states.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of O(m+n) entry points, the light path visits at most O(m*n) cells.
space_complexity_explanation: Visited set can store up to O(m*n) states per path simulation.
key_insight: Simulate each light path from border entry points, reflecting direction based on mirror type, and track visited states to detect cycles.

title: Minimize Malware Spread
hints: Find connected components using Union-Find or DFS.
A component is only saved if exactly one initial infected node is in it.
Remove the node whose sole presence infects the largest component.
approach: Use Union-Find to identify connected components. For each component, count how many initially infected nodes it contains. If a component has exactly one infected node, removing that node saves the entire component. Pick the node that saves the most nodes, breaking ties by smallest index.
time_complexity_explanation: Building the union-find from the adjacency matrix takes O(n^2).
space_complexity_explanation: Union-Find parent and size arrays of size n.
key_insight: Only components with exactly one infected node benefit from removal; removing that node saves the entire component from infection.

title: Convert Integer to the Sum of Two No-Zero Integers
hints: Try all pairs (a, n-a) where a goes from 1 to n-1.
Check if both numbers contain no zero digit.
Convert to string and check for '0'.
approach: Iterate a from 1 to n-1. For each a, check if neither a nor n-a contains the digit 0. Return the first valid pair found.
time_complexity_explanation: Try up to n values; checking digits of each takes O(log n).
space_complexity_explanation: Only constant extra space used besides the output.
key_insight: Brute force works well here since a valid pair is found very quickly — typically within the first few iterations.

title: Number of People Aware of a Secret
hints: Track how many people learn the secret on each day.
A person who learned on day i can share from day i+delay to day i+forget-1.
People forget after exactly 'forget' days from when they learned.
approach: Use a DP array where dp[i] = number of people who learn the secret on day i. On day 1, dp[1] = 1. For each subsequent day, sum up dp[j] for all j where the person learned during [i-forget+1, i-delay]. The answer is the sum of dp[i] for days in [n-forget+1, n].
time_complexity_explanation: For each of n days, we look back up to 'forget' days to sum sharing people.
space_complexity_explanation: DP array of size n+1.
key_insight: Track new learners per day: people who learned on day j can share on days [j+delay, j+forget-1] and forget on day j+forget.

title: Rearrange Words in a Sentence
hints: Split the sentence into words and sort by word length.
Python's sort is stable, so words of equal length keep their original order.
Capitalize the first word and lowercase the rest.
approach: Split the sentence into words, convert all to lowercase, sort by length (stable sort preserves original order for ties), then capitalize the first word and join.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting the words array where n is the number of words.
space_complexity_explanation: Storing the split words and result string.
key_insight: Use a stable sort by word length so that words of equal length retain their original relative order from the sentence.

title: Text Justification
hints: Greedily pack as many words as possible into each line.
Distribute extra spaces as evenly as possible, with more spaces on the left.
The last line should be left-justified with single spaces.
approach: Greedily fit words into lines. For each line (except the last), distribute spaces evenly between words, giving extra spaces to the left slots. The last line is left-justified with a single space between words, padded with trailing spaces.
time_complexity_explanation: Each word is processed exactly once when building lines.
space_complexity_explanation: Output stores all words distributed across lines.
key_insight: Pack words greedily into lines, then distribute extra spaces left-to-right among gaps; handle the last line and single-word lines as left-justified.

title: Vowel Spellchecker
hints: Check three levels of matching: exact, case-insensitive, vowel-replaced.
Build lookup maps for case-insensitive and vowel-pattern matching.
Replace all vowels with a wildcard to create a canonical form for vowel matching.
approach: Build three data structures: a set for exact matches, a map from lowercased words to the first matching wordlist entry, and a map from vowel-replaced patterns to the first match. For each query, check exact match first, then case-insensitive, then vowel pattern.
time_complexity_explanation: Building maps from wordlist (n) and processing queries (m), each in O(word_length).
space_complexity_explanation: Three lookup structures each storing up to n entries.
key_insight: Build three tiers of lookup: exact set, case-insensitive map, and vowel-agnostic map (replacing vowels with wildcards), checking each query against tiers in priority order.

title: Robot Room Cleaner
hints: Use DFS with backtracking — clean current cell, then try all 4 directions.
Track visited cells using relative coordinates from the starting position.
To backtrack, turn around (two right turns), move forward, then turn around again.
approach: Perform DFS from the starting position. At each cell, clean it and mark as visited using relative coordinates. Try moving in all 4 directions. If a direction is valid (move succeeds and cell not visited), recurse. After exploring, backtrack by turning 180 degrees, moving forward, and turning 180 degrees again.
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def cleanRoom(self, robot) -> None:
        directions = [(-1, 0), (0, 1), (1, 0), (0, -1)]
        visited = set()
        def goBack():
            robot.turnRight()
            robot.turnRight()
            robot.move()
            robot.turnRight()
            robot.turnRight()
        def dfs(x, y, d):
            visited.add((x, y))
            robot.clean()
            for i in range(4):
                nd = (d + i) % 4
                nx, ny = x + directions[nd][0], y + directions[nd][1]
                if (nx, ny) not in visited and robot.move():
                    dfs(nx, ny, nd)
                    goBack()
                robot.turnRight()
        dfs(0, 0, 0)
time_complexity_explanation: Visit each accessible cell exactly once where n is total cells and m is obstacles.
space_complexity_explanation: Visited set and recursion stack proportional to accessible cells.
key_insight: Use relative coordinates from the start position to track visited cells, and always backtrack by turning 180°, moving, and turning 180° again to restore the robot's position and direction.

title: Average Time of Process per Machine
hints: Self-join the Activity table on machine_id and process_id to pair start and end events.
Calculate the difference between end and start timestamps.
Group by machine_id and average the processing times.
approach: Self-join Activity table matching start and end events for the same machine and process. Compute the time difference for each process, then average per machine, rounded to 3 decimals.
solution_python:
SELECT a1.machine_id, ROUND(AVG(a2.timestamp - a1.timestamp), 3) AS processing_time
FROM Activity a1
JOIN Activity a2
  ON a1.machine_id = a2.machine_id
  AND a1.process_id = a2.process_id
  AND a1.activity_type = 'start'
  AND a2.activity_type = 'end'
GROUP BY a1.machine_id;
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: Self-join on indexed columns with grouping over n activity rows.
space_complexity_explanation: Intermediate join results proportional to the number of processes.
key_insight: Self-join pairing start and end activity types on the same machine and process gives you each process duration directly.

title: Maximum Number of Words You Can Type
hints: Convert brokenLetters into a set for O(1) lookup.
Split the text into words and check each word.
A word can be typed if none of its characters are broken.
approach: Create a set of broken letters. Split text into words. Count words where no character is in the broken set.
time_complexity_explanation: Scan through all characters in the text once.
space_complexity_explanation: Broken letters set has at most 26 entries, which is constant.
key_insight: A word is typable if and only if the intersection of its characters with the broken letters set is empty.

title: Battleships in a Board
hints: Count only the top-left cell of each battleship.
A cell is a battleship head if there's no 'X' above it and no 'X' to its left.
This gives an O(1) space single-pass solution without modifying the board.
approach: Iterate through the board. Count a cell with 'X' only if the cell above and the cell to the left are not 'X'. This counts each battleship exactly once at its top-left corner.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the entire board.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a counter variable, no extra data structures.
key_insight: Count only the top-left corner of each battleship by checking that no 'X' exists directly above or to the left.

title: Design Task Manager
hints: Use a max-heap (priority queue) keyed by priority then task ID.
Maintain a hash map from task ID to (user, priority) for O(1) lookups.
Use lazy deletion: mark removed tasks and skip them when popping from heap.
approach: Use a max-heap storing (-priority, -taskId, userId) for the execTop operation. Maintain a dict mapping taskId to (userId, priority). For add/edit, update the dict and push to heap. For rmv, delete from dict. For execTop, pop from heap until finding a valid (still in dict) entry.
time_complexity_explanation: Each add/edit/execTop operation is O(log n) for heap push/pop. rmv is O(1) with lazy deletion.
space_complexity_explanation: Heap and hash map store all tasks, possibly with stale entries.
key_insight: Use lazy deletion with a max-heap: push new entries on edit and skip stale entries when popping, validating against the current task map.

title: Minimum Operations to Make Array Equal to Target
hints: Compute the difference array diff[i] = target[i] - nums[i].
The problem reduces to making the diff array all zeros using increment/decrement on subarrays.
This is equivalent to counting the total positive increases and total negative decreases in consecutive differences.
approach: Compute diff = target[i] - nums[i] for each i. The minimum operations equals the sum of all positive 'upward steps' plus the absolute sum of all negative 'downward steps' when scanning the diff array from left to right. When diff changes sign, the full absolute value is added.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the arrays to compute differences and accumulate operations.
space_complexity_explanation: Difference array of size n.
key_insight: The minimum operations equal the sum of all 'new work' needed at each position: when the difference increases (for positive diffs) or decreases (for negative diffs), that delta represents an additional operation.

title: Maximal Network Rank
hints: The network rank of two nodes is the total number of edges connected to either node.
If the two nodes are directly connected, subtract 1 to avoid double-counting.
Precompute the degree of each node and check all pairs.
approach: Compute the degree of each node and store edges in a set. For each pair (i, j), their network rank is degree[i] + degree[j] - (1 if edge (i,j) exists else 0). Return the maximum.
time_complexity_explanation: Check all O(n^2) pairs of nodes with O(1) edge lookup.
space_complexity_explanation: Degree array of size n and edge set of size E (number of roads).
key_insight: Network rank of a pair is simply the sum of their degrees minus 1 if they share a direct edge, to avoid double-counting.

title: Count Elements With Maximum Frequency
hints: Count the frequency of each element using a hash map.
Find the maximum frequency among all elements.
Sum up the frequencies of all elements that have the maximum frequency.
approach: Use a hash map to count frequencies of each element. Find the maximum frequency, then sum the frequencies of all elements that appear with that maximum frequency.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through the array once to build the frequency map and once more through the map values.
space_complexity_explanation: The hash map stores up to n distinct elements in the worst case.
key_insight: Count frequencies, find the max frequency, then sum all frequencies equal to that max.

title: Making A Large Island
hints: Label each island with a unique ID and record its size.
For each 0-cell, check the sizes of distinct neighboring islands.
The answer is the max of (1 + sum of distinct neighbor island sizes) over all 0-cells, or the whole grid if no 0 exists.
approach: DFS to label each connected component with a unique island ID and store its size. Then for each 0-cell, look at the 4 neighbors and sum the sizes of distinct adjacent islands plus 1. Track the global maximum.
time_complexity_explanation: We visit each cell a constant number of times during DFS labeling and the second pass.
space_complexity_explanation: The recursion stack and the size map can use up to O(n^2) space.
key_insight: Label each island with DFS, then for each zero cell check which distinct islands are adjacent and sum their sizes.

title: Minimum Initial Energy to Finish Tasks
hints: Think about the difference between the minimum energy required and the actual energy spent for each task.
Sort tasks by the difference (minimum - actual) in descending order.
Greedily simulate the process to compute the minimum starting energy.
approach: Sort tasks by (minimum - actual) in descending order. Then simulate: track the running energy and ensure we always have enough to meet each task's minimum requirement, accumulating the total initial energy needed.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting dominates at O(n log n), followed by a single linear pass.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a few variables are used beyond the input array (sorted in-place).
key_insight: Sort by (minimum - actual) ascending so tasks with the largest gap between threshold and cost are done last, minimizing wasted energy.

title: Wildcard Matching
hints: '?' matches exactly one character, '*' matches any sequence (including empty).
Use DP where dp[i][j] means pattern[:i] matches string[:j].
For '*', it either matches empty (dp[i-1][j]) or extends a match (dp[i][j-1]).
approach: Use 2D DP where dp[i][j] indicates whether pattern[0..i-1] matches s[0..j-1]. Handle '?' as single char match, '*' as matching empty or one more character from s.
time_complexity_explanation: We fill an m x n DP table where m is the string length and n is the pattern length.
space_complexity_explanation: The 2D DP table uses O(m * n) space.
key_insight: For '*', it either matches nothing (look up) or extends to match one more character from s (look left in DP table).

title: Kth Missing Positive Number
hints: The number of positive integers missing before arr[i] is arr[i] - (i + 1).
Use binary search to find the position where the count of missing numbers reaches k.
The answer is lo + k where lo is the binary search result.
approach: Binary search on the array. At index i, the number of missing positives before arr[i] is arr[i] - (i+1). Find the first index where this count >= k. The kth missing number is then k + lo.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search over the array of length n.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used.
key_insight: At index i, the count of missing numbers before arr[i] is arr[i] - (i+1); binary search for where this reaches k.

title: Count Subarrays With Fixed Bounds
hints: Track the last positions of minK, maxK, and any out-of-range element.
For each index, valid subarrays start after the last bad element and must include both the last minK and last maxK.
The count contribution at each index is max(0, min(lastMin, lastMax) - lastBad).
approach: Scan left to right tracking the last index of minK, maxK, and the last out-of-bounds element. At each position, the number of valid subarrays ending here is max(0, min(lastMin, lastMax) - lastBad).
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the array.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a few tracking variables are used.
key_insight: At each index, count valid subarrays ending here using the last positions of minK, maxK, and the last out-of-range element.

title: Minimum Score Triangulation of Polygon
hints: Think of the polygon vertices in order; consider fixing one edge and choosing the third vertex of a triangle.
Use interval DP: dp[i][j] = minimum cost to triangulate the sub-polygon from vertex i to j.
For each k between i and j, dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][k] + dp[k][j] + v[i]*v[k]*v[j]).
approach: Interval DP where dp[i][j] represents the minimum triangulation cost for vertices i..j. For each pair (i,j), try every vertex k in between as the triangle apex with edge (i,j).
time_complexity_explanation: Three nested loops: two for the interval endpoints and one for the split point k.
space_complexity_explanation: The 2D DP table of size n x n.
key_insight: Fix edge (i, j) and try every vertex k between them as the triangle apex; this decomposes into two smaller sub-polygons.

title: Count the Number of Infection Sequences
hints: Infected children split the row into gaps. Each gap can be infected independently.
A gap of length g in the middle has 2^(g-1) orderings; edge gaps have 1 ordering each.
Use multinomial coefficients to combine the independent orderings of all gaps.
approach: Find the gaps between infected positions. Middle gaps of size g contribute 2^(g-1) orderings, edge gaps contribute 1. Combine using multinomial coefficient: (total_uninfected)! / product(gap_i!) * product of gap orderings, all mod 10^9+7.
time_complexity_explanation: Computing factorials and iterating through gaps are both linear in n.
space_complexity_explanation: Factorial and inverse factorial arrays of size up to n.
key_insight: Gaps between infected positions can be filled independently; middle gaps have 2^(g-1) orderings, combined via multinomial coefficients.

title: Intersection of Two Arrays II
hints: Count the frequency of each element in one array using a hash map.
Iterate through the second array and collect common elements by decrementing counts.
Alternatively, sort both arrays and use two pointers.
approach: Count element frequencies in the smaller array using a hash map. Iterate through the other array; for each element found in the map with count > 0, add it to the result and decrement the count.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through both arrays once each.
space_complexity_explanation: The hash map stores at most min(m, n) entries.
key_insight: Use a frequency counter on one array and decrement as you find matches in the second array.

title: Product Sales Analysis III
hints: Find the first year each product was sold.
Use a subquery or window function to get the minimum year per product.
Join back to get the full row for that first year.
approach: Use a subquery to find the minimum year for each product_id, then join back to the Sales table to get the quantity and price for that first year.
solution_python:
SELECT s.product_id, s.year AS first_year, s.quantity, s.price
FROM Sales s
INNER JOIN (
    SELECT product_id, MIN(year) AS first_year
    FROM Sales
    GROUP BY product_id
) t ON s.product_id = t.product_id AND s.year = t.first_year;
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The query scans the Sales table and groups by product_id, which is linear in the number of rows.
space_complexity_explanation: The subquery result set is proportional to the number of distinct products.
key_insight: Find the minimum year per product with GROUP BY, then join back to get the full row details.

title: Sorting the Sentence
hints: Each word has a position digit appended at the end.
Extract the digit and the word part separately.
Place each word at its correct position and join with spaces.
approach: Split the sentence into words. For each word, the last character is the 1-based position. Strip the digit, place the word in the correct index, and join.
time_complexity_explanation: We process each word once, where n is the total length of the string.
space_complexity_explanation: We store the result array of words.
key_insight: The last character of each word is its 1-based position in the original sentence.

title: Binary Subarrays With Sum
hints: Use prefix sums: count subarrays with sum exactly goal.
A hash map can track how many times each prefix sum has occurred.
Alternatively, use the at-most-k trick: atMost(goal) - atMost(goal - 1).
approach: Use prefix sum with a hash map. For each prefix sum, the number of subarrays ending here with sum equal to goal is the count of previous prefix sums equal to (current - goal).
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def numSubarraysWithSum(self, nums: List[int], goal: int) -> int:
        count = defaultdict(int)
        count[0] = 1
        prefix = 0
        res = 0
        for x in nums:
            prefix += x
            res += count[prefix - goal]
            count[prefix] += 1
        return res
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the array with O(1) hash map lookups.
space_complexity_explanation: The hash map stores up to n+1 distinct prefix sums.
key_insight: Prefix sum + hash map: for each position, count how many earlier prefix sums equal (current prefix sum - goal).

title: Successful Pairs of Spells and Potions
hints: Sort the potions array first.
For each spell, binary search for the minimum potion strength needed.
The number of successful pairs is the count of potions at or above that threshold.
approach: Sort potions. For each spell, binary search for the smallest potion value such that spell * potion >= success. The count of valid potions is len(potions) - that index.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting potions is O(n log n), then for each of m spells we do a binary search O(log n).
space_complexity_explanation: The result array of size m. Sorting is in-place.
key_insight: Sort potions once, then for each spell binary search for the minimum potion that satisfies the success threshold.

title: Length of Last Word
hints: Trim trailing spaces first.
Scan backwards from the end to find the last word.
Count characters until you hit a space or reach the beginning.
approach: Strip trailing spaces, then iterate backwards counting characters until a space is found.
time_complexity_explanation: We may scan the entire string in the worst case.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a few variables are used (ignoring the split which can be avoided with a pointer approach).
key_insight: Trim trailing spaces, then find the length from the last space to the end.

title: Design Memory Allocator
hints: Use an array to simulate memory blocks.
For allocate, scan for the first contiguous block of free units of the required size.
For free, scan the entire array and free all units with the given mID.
approach: Maintain an array of size n representing memory. For allocate(size, mID), find the first contiguous run of 0s of length >= size and fill with mID. For free(mID), set all cells with that mID back to 0 and count them.
time_complexity_explanation: Both allocate and free scan the entire memory array in the worst case.
space_complexity_explanation: The memory array of size n.
key_insight: Simulate memory as an array; allocate finds the first-fit contiguous block, free clears all blocks matching the ID.

title: Find K Pairs with Smallest Sums
hints: Use a min-heap to always extract the pair with the smallest sum.
Start by pushing (nums1[i] + nums2[0], i, 0) for the first min(k, len(nums1)) elements.
For each extracted pair (i, j), push the next pair (i, j+1) if it exists.
approach: Min-heap approach: initialize with pairs (nums1[i], nums2[0]) for the first min(k, len(nums1)) indices. Pop the smallest, add to result, then push the pair with the next element in nums2.
time_complexity_explanation: We perform at most k heap operations, each O(log k).
space_complexity_explanation: The heap holds at most k elements.
key_insight: Use a min-heap seeded with each nums1 element paired with nums2[0], then lazily expand by incrementing the nums2 index.

title: Minimum Number of Refueling Stops
hints: Think greedily: drive as far as possible, then refuel at the best station you passed.
Use a max-heap to store fuel amounts from stations you have passed.
When you run out of fuel, pop from the heap to refuel with the largest available amount.
approach: Greedily drive forward. As you pass stations, add their fuel to a max-heap. When you can't reach the next station (or target), pop the largest fuel from the heap. Count the number of pops.
time_complexity_explanation: Each station is pushed and potentially popped from the heap once, each O(log n).
space_complexity_explanation: The max-heap can hold up to n station fuel amounts.
key_insight: Greedily pass stations adding fuel to a max-heap; when you run out, refuel from the largest passed station.

title: Check if the Sentence Is Pangram
hints: A pangram contains every letter of the alphabet at least once.
Use a set to track unique letters seen.
Check if the set size is 26.
approach: Add all characters to a set and check if it contains all 26 lowercase letters.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through the string once.
space_complexity_explanation: The set holds at most 26 characters, which is constant.
key_insight: A sentence is a pangram if and only if the set of its characters has size 26.

title: Broken Calculator
hints: Think backwards: instead of going from startValue to target, reverse from target to startValue.
If target is odd, the last operation must have been subtract 1, so add 1.
If target is even, the last operation could have been multiply by 2, so divide by 2.
approach: Work backwards from target. If target > startValue: if target is odd, add 1 (reverse of subtract); if even, divide by 2 (reverse of multiply). When target <= startValue, add the difference.
time_complexity_explanation: Each even step halves the target, so we do at most O(log target) iterations.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a few variables are used.
key_insight: Work backwards: dividing an even target by 2 is much more efficient than doubling forward; odd targets need +1 first.

title: Concatenation of Array
hints: The result array has length 2n where n is the input length.
For index i in result: ans[i] = nums[i % n].
Or simply concatenate the array with itself.
approach: Return the array concatenated with itself. In Python, nums + nums. In JS, [...nums, ...nums].
time_complexity_explanation: We copy n elements twice to form the result of size 2n.
space_complexity_explanation: The output array is of size 2n.
key_insight: Simply concatenate the array with itself to get the answer.

title: Minimum Genetic Mutation
hints: Think of each gene string as a node in a graph and each valid single-character mutation as an edge.
Use BFS to find the shortest path from the start gene to the end gene.
Only mutations that exist in the gene bank are valid next steps.
approach: Use BFS starting from the start gene. At each step, try replacing each character with one of 'A', 'C', 'G', 'T'. If the resulting gene is in the bank and not yet visited, add it to the queue. Return the number of steps when the end gene is reached, or -1 if unreachable.
time_complexity_explanation: We may visit every gene in the bank (B), and for each gene we try 4 mutations at each of L positions.
space_complexity_explanation: The visited set and queue can hold at most all genes in the bank.
key_insight: Model the problem as a shortest-path search in an unweighted graph where nodes are valid gene strings and edges are single-character mutations.

title: Investments in 2016
hints: A policyholder qualifies if their tiv_2015 value is shared by at least one other policyholder.
A policyholder also must have a unique (lat, lon) location pair.
Use subqueries or GROUP BY/HAVING to filter each condition independently.
approach: Select the sum of tiv_2016 where the tiv_2015 value appears more than once (shared with another policyholder) AND the (lat, lon) pair is unique (appears exactly once).
solution_python:
SELECT ROUND(SUM(tiv_2016), 2) AS tiv_2016
FROM Insurance
WHERE tiv_2015 IN (
    SELECT tiv_2015
    FROM Insurance
    GROUP BY tiv_2015
    HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
)
AND (lat, lon) IN (
    SELECT lat, lon
    FROM Insurance
    GROUP BY lat, lon
    HAVING COUNT(*) = 1
);
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The query scans the table multiple times with GROUP BY operations, each linear in the number of rows.
space_complexity_explanation: Intermediate grouping results require space proportional to the number of distinct tiv_2015 values and location pairs.
key_insight: Combine two independent filters: tiv_2015 must be non-unique (shared) while the geographic location must be unique.

title: Maximum OR
hints: Doubling a number (left shift by 1) is always better applied to one number rather than spreading shifts across multiple numbers.
All k operations should be applied to a single element to maximize the OR.
Use prefix and suffix OR arrays to quickly compute the OR of all other elements.
approach: Since applying all k left shifts to a single element maximizes the result, compute prefix and suffix OR arrays. For each element, calculate (prefix_or | (nums[i] << k) | suffix_or) and take the maximum.
time_complexity_explanation: We make two linear passes: one to build the suffix OR array and one to compute the answer.
space_complexity_explanation: The suffix OR array uses O(n) space.
key_insight: All k multiplications should be applied to a single element because left-shifting one number sets higher bits than splitting shifts across numbers.

title: Excel Sheet Column Title
hints: This is similar to converting a number to base-26, but the digits range from 1 to 26 instead of 0 to 25.
Subtract 1 before taking the modulus to handle the 1-indexed nature of the encoding.
Build the result string from right to left and reverse at the end.
approach: Repeatedly subtract 1 from the column number (to make it 0-indexed), take mod 26 to get the current letter, prepend it to the result, then divide by 26. Continue until the number reaches 0.
time_complexity_explanation: We divide the column number by 26 in each iteration, so the loop runs O(log26 n) times.
space_complexity_explanation: The result string length is proportional to log26 of the column number.
key_insight: Subtracting 1 before each mod operation converts the 1-indexed system (A=1, Z=26) into a 0-indexed base-26 system.

title: Assign Cookies
hints: Sort both the greed factors and cookie sizes so you can match the smallest available cookie to the least greedy child.
Use two pointers: one for children and one for cookies.
If a cookie can satisfy the current child, move both pointers; otherwise, skip the cookie.
approach: Sort both arrays. Use two pointers to greedily assign the smallest sufficient cookie to each child. If the current cookie satisfies the child, increment both pointers and the count; otherwise, move to the next cookie.
time_complexity: O(n log n + m log m)
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting both arrays dominates. The two-pointer scan is O(n + m).
space_complexity_explanation: Sorting is done in-place and only a constant number of variables are used.
key_insight: Sorting enables a greedy strategy: always try the smallest available cookie first, since using a larger cookie than necessary wastes resources.

title: Consecutive Numbers Sum
hints: A sum of k consecutive integers starting from x is: k*x + k*(k-1)/2 = n.
Rearrange to x = (n - k*(k-1)/2) / k, which must be a positive integer.
Iterate over possible values of k while k*(k-1)/2 < n.
approach: For k consecutive numbers starting at x: n = k*x + k*(k-1)/2. So x = (n - k*(k-1)/2)/k. Iterate k from 1 upward while k*(k-1)/2 < n, and count how many k values give a positive integer x.
time_complexity_explanation: The loop runs while k*(k-1)/2 < n, meaning k goes up to roughly sqrt(2n), so about O(sqrt(n)) iterations.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used.
key_insight: The sum of k consecutive integers starting at x equals k*x + k*(k-1)/2, so check if n minus the triangular number is evenly divisible by k.

title: Minimum Number of Steps to Make Two Strings Anagram
hints: Count the frequency of each character in both strings.
For each character, if t has fewer occurrences than s, those extras in s must be replaced.
The answer is the sum of positive differences in character counts.
approach: Count character frequencies for both strings. For each character, compute the difference count_t[c] - count_s[c]. Sum up all positive differences (characters that need to be added to t), which equals the number of replacements needed in t.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through both strings once to count frequencies, then iterate through 26 letters.
space_complexity_explanation: The frequency arrays have a fixed size of 26 regardless of input length.
key_insight: The minimum replacements needed equals the total surplus of characters that s has over t, since each surplus character must be changed to a deficit character.

title: Create Hello World Function
hints: You need to return a function, not a string.
The returned function should ignore all arguments passed to it.
Use a closure or simply return a function that always returns 'Hello World'.
approach: Return a function that, regardless of any arguments passed to it, always returns the string 'Hello World'.
time_complexity_explanation: The function simply returns a constant string with no computation.
space_complexity_explanation: No additional data structures are created.
key_insight: Higher-order functions can return other functions; the inner function forms a closure but here simply returns a constant.

title: Employee Bonus
hints: Use a LEFT JOIN to include employees who have no bonus record.
Employees with no bonus should also appear in results since NULL < 1000 is not true, so check IS NULL explicitly.
Filter where bonus < 1000 OR bonus IS NULL.
approach: LEFT JOIN the Employee table with the Bonus table on empId. Filter rows where the bonus is less than 1000 or is NULL (no bonus record exists).
solution_python:
SELECT e.name, b.bonus
FROM Employee e
LEFT JOIN Bonus b ON e.empId = b.empId
WHERE b.bonus < 1000 OR b.bonus IS NULL;
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The query performs a single join and filter across all employee rows.
space_complexity_explanation: The result set can contain up to all employees.
key_insight: LEFT JOIN preserves employees without a bonus entry, and the WHERE clause must explicitly handle NULL values since comparisons with NULL return unknown.

title: Counter
hints: Use a closure to maintain state between function calls.
The returned function should return the current value and then increment it.
Initialize the counter with the given starting value n.
approach: Return a closure that captures the initial value n. Each time the returned function is called, return the current value and then increment it for the next call.
time_complexity_explanation: Each call simply returns and increments a single variable.
space_complexity_explanation: The closure captures only one variable regardless of how many times it is called.
key_insight: Closures capture variables by reference, so the inner function can read and modify n across multiple invocations.

title: Calculate Money in Leetcode Bank
hints: Each complete week deposits 1+2+3+4+5+6+7 = 28, plus an extra 7 for each subsequent week.
The k-th complete week deposits 28 + 7*(k-1).
Handle the remaining days after the last complete week separately.
approach: Calculate complete weeks and remaining days. Each complete week k (0-indexed) deposits 28 + 7*k. The remaining days in the partial week start at (weeks + 1) and increment by 1 each day. Sum both parts using arithmetic series formulas.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate once per week plus once per remaining day, totaling at most n/7 + 7 iterations.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used.
key_insight: Each week's deposit forms an arithmetic sequence starting at 28 with a common difference of 7, and remaining days also form a simple arithmetic sequence.

title: Design Circular Queue
hints: Use a fixed-size array with head and tail pointers that wrap around using modulo arithmetic.
Track the current count of elements to distinguish between full and empty states.
Enqueue adds at tail, dequeue removes from head — both advance their pointer with modulo.
approach: Use a fixed-size array with front pointer, and a count to track the number of elements. Enqueue inserts at (front + count) % k, dequeue advances front by 1 mod k. Use count to check full/empty conditions.
time_complexity_explanation: All operations (enqueue, dequeue, front, rear, isEmpty, isFull) run in constant time.
space_complexity_explanation: The internal array has a fixed size of k elements.
key_insight: Using a count variable instead of comparing head and tail pointers elegantly distinguishes between full and empty states in a circular buffer.

title: Smallest String With Swaps
hints: If index a can swap with b, and b can swap with c, then all three indices can be rearranged freely among themselves.
Use Union-Find to group all transitively connected indices into components.
Within each component, sort the characters and assign them to the sorted indices for the lexicographically smallest result.
approach: Use Union-Find to group indices connected by swap pairs. For each connected component, collect the characters and their indices, sort both, and place the sorted characters at the sorted indices.
time_complexity_explanation: Union-Find operations are near-constant per pair (E pairs). Sorting characters within components is O(n log n) in the worst case.
space_complexity_explanation: The parent array, groups map, and result array all use O(n) space.
key_insight: Transitive swap relationships form connected components; within each component, characters can be freely rearranged, so sorting them gives the lexicographically smallest result.

title: N-Queens II
hints: Place queens row by row, and for each row try every column.
Track which columns, diagonals, and anti-diagonals are already attacked.
Use sets for O(1) conflict checking: columns, (row-col) diagonals, and (row+col) anti-diagonals.
