approach: Use backtracking to place one queen per row. Maintain sets for occupied columns, main diagonals (row - col), and anti-diagonals (row + col). For each row, try each column; if no conflict, place the queen and recurse. Count solutions when all rows are filled.
time_complexity_explanation: In the worst case we explore n choices for the first row, n-1 for the second, etc., giving O(n!) placements to check.
space_complexity_explanation: The recursion depth is n, and the three sets each hold at most n elements.
key_insight: Encoding diagonal and anti-diagonal attacks as (row - col) and (row + col) allows O(1) conflict detection during backtracking.

title: Count Negative Numbers in a Sorted Matrix
hints: Each row is sorted in non-increasing order, so you can binary search for the first negative number.
Alternatively, start from the top-right corner and use the staircase pattern.
Moving left skips non-negatives; moving down adds negatives from the current boundary.
approach: Start from the top-right corner. If the current element is negative, all elements below it in that column are also negative — add them to count and move left. Otherwise, move down. This traces the boundary in O(m + n) time.
time_complexity_explanation: We move at most m steps down and n steps left, for a total of O(m + n) moves.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used.
key_insight: The staircase traversal from the top-right corner exploits the sorted property in both directions to count negatives without examining every element.

title: Evaluate Division
hints: Model the equations as a weighted directed graph: a/b = val means an edge from a to b with weight val, and b to a with weight 1/val.
For each query a/c, find a path from a to c and multiply the edge weights along the path.
Use BFS or DFS to find the path; if no path exists, return -1.0.
approach: Build a weighted graph from the equations. For each query, use BFS to find a path from the dividend to the divisor, multiplying edge weights along the way. If either variable is unknown or no path exists, return -1.0.
time_complexity_explanation: Each query runs a BFS that may visit all vertices and edges in the graph. Q is the number of queries.
space_complexity_explanation: The graph stores all variables as vertices and all equations as edges.
key_insight: Division equations form a weighted graph where path products give transitive division results — a/c = (a/b) * (b/c).

title: Reorganize String
hints: If any character appears more than (n+1)/2 times, it's impossible to reorganize.
Use a max-heap to always place the most frequent remaining character next.
After placing a character, hold it aside for one turn to prevent consecutive placement.
approach: Count character frequencies. Use a max-heap to always pick the most frequent character. After placing a character, save it and only push it back after placing a different character. This ensures no two adjacent characters are the same.
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def reorganizeString(self, s: str) -> str:
        from collections import Counter
        import heapq
        counts = Counter(s)
        max_heap = [(-cnt, ch) for ch, cnt in counts.items()]
        heapq.heapify(max_heap)
        result = []
        prev_cnt, prev_ch = 0, ''
        while max_heap:
            cnt, ch = heapq.heappop(max_heap)
            if prev_cnt < 0:
                heapq.heappush(max_heap, (prev_cnt, prev_ch))
            result.append(ch)
            prev_cnt, prev_ch = cnt + 1, ch
        return ''.join(result) if len(result) == len(s) else ''
time_complexity_explanation: We process n characters, and each heap operation takes O(log k) where k is the number of distinct characters (at most 26).
space_complexity_explanation: The heap and frequency map hold at most k = 26 distinct characters.
key_insight: Greedily placing the most frequent character that differs from the previous one ensures no adjacent duplicates whenever a valid arrangement exists.

title: Smallest Number With All Set Bits
hints: A number with all set bits is of the form 2^k - 1 (e.g., 1, 3, 7, 15, 31, ...).
Find the smallest k such that 2^k - 1 >= n.
Alternatively, find the bit length of n and return 2^(bit_length) - 1.
approach: Find the number of bits needed to represent n, then return a number with all those bits set: (1 << bit_length) - 1. This is the smallest all-set-bits number >= n.
time_complexity_explanation: Finding the bit length requires O(log n) operations (or O(1) with built-in methods).
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used.
key_insight: The smallest number >= n with all bits set is always of the form 2^k - 1, where k is the bit length of n.

title: Insert into a Binary Search Tree
hints: The new node should always be inserted as a leaf to maintain BST validity.
Compare the value with the current node to decide whether to go left or right.
Recursively or iteratively traverse until you find a null spot.
approach: Recursively traverse the BST. If the current node is null, create and return a new node with the given value. Otherwise, compare the value with the current node and recurse into the left or right subtree accordingly.
time_complexity_explanation: We traverse from root to a leaf, visiting at most h nodes where h is the tree height.
space_complexity_explanation: The recursion stack depth equals the tree height h, which is O(log n) for balanced trees and O(n) worst case.
key_insight: BST insertion always places the new value at a leaf position found by following the BST ordering property from root to null.

title: The Two Sneaky Numbers of Digitville
hints: The array contains numbers 0 to n-1 with exactly two duplicates.
Use a set to track seen numbers; if a number is already in the set, it's a duplicate.
Alternatively, use a frequency count and find numbers with count 2.
approach: Iterate through the array while maintaining a set of seen numbers. Whenever a number is already in the set, add it to the result. Return the two duplicate numbers found.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through the array once, and set lookups are O(1) amortized.
space_complexity_explanation: The set can hold up to n elements in the worst case.
key_insight: A hash set provides O(1) duplicate detection — the first time a number is seen twice, it's identified as sneaky.

title: Flatten a Multilevel Doubly Linked List
hints: Think of the child pointer as a branching path you need to explore before continuing.
Use a stack or recursion to handle each child level, then reconnect the next pointers.
When you encounter a child, insert the child chain between the current node and current.next.
approach: Iterate through the list. When a node has a child, recursively flatten the child list, then splice it between the current node and current.next. Clear the child pointer after flattening.
time_complexity_explanation: Each node is visited at most twice: once during the main traversal and once when finding the tail of a child list.
space_complexity_explanation: The iterative approach uses only a constant number of pointers.
key_insight: When a child is found, find the tail of the child chain, splice it between current and current.next, then continue the main traversal through the now-flattened list.

title: Design In-Memory File System
hints: Model the file system as a trie where each node represents a directory or file.
Each node can store children (subdirectories/files) in a hash map and optionally file content.
For ls, if the path is a file return just the file name; if a directory, return sorted children.
approach: Use a trie-like structure where each node has a dictionary of children and an optional content string. Navigate the trie by splitting paths on '/'. ls returns sorted children or file name, mkdir creates nodes along the path, addContentToFile appends content, and readContentFromFile returns stored content.
time_complexity_explanation: m is the length of the path (for navigation). For ls, k log k for sorting k entries in a directory. Other operations are O(m).
space_complexity_explanation: O(n) where n is the total number of files and directories stored in the trie.
key_insight: A trie naturally models a hierarchical file system where each node represents a directory or file, and children map names to sub-nodes.

title: Remove Linked List Elements
hints: Use a dummy/sentinel node before the head to handle edge cases where the head itself needs removal.
Iterate with a pointer; if the next node's value matches, skip it by relinking.
Return dummy.next as the new head.
approach: Create a sentinel node pointing to head. Iterate through the list: if the next node has the target value, skip it; otherwise advance. Return sentinel.next.
time_complexity_explanation: We traverse each node in the linked list exactly once.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of pointers are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: A sentinel/dummy node before head elegantly handles the case where the head itself needs to be removed.

title: Sort Characters By Frequency
hints: Count the frequency of each character using a hash map.
Sort characters by their frequency in descending order.
Build the result string by repeating each character by its frequency.
approach: Count character frequencies with a hash map, then sort by frequency descending. Build the result by repeating each character its frequency number of times.
time_complexity_explanation: Counting is O(n), sorting the unique characters is O(k log k) where k <= n, and building the result is O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: The frequency map and the result string each take O(n) space.
key_insight: Count frequencies then sort by frequency descending; Python's Counter.most_common() does both steps conveniently.

title: Find if Path Exists in Graph
hints: Build an adjacency list from the edges.
Use BFS or DFS from the source to see if you can reach the destination.
Alternatively, use Union-Find to check if source and destination are in the same connected component.
approach: Build an adjacency list, then perform BFS/DFS from source. If destination is visited, return true; otherwise return false.
time_complexity_explanation: BFS visits each vertex and edge at most once.
space_complexity_explanation: The adjacency list stores all edges and the visited set stores all vertices.
key_insight: A simple BFS or DFS from source determines reachability to destination in an undirected graph.

title: Power Grid Maintenance
hints: Model the grid as a graph where nodes are power stations and edges are connections.
Think about what happens when you remove a node — which other nodes lose connectivity?
Use BFS/DFS to check connectivity after each maintenance operation.
approach: For each maintenance query, temporarily remove the node from the graph, then use BFS/DFS to count how many nodes remain connected to the power source. The difference gives the number of affected stations.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of q queries, we perform a BFS/DFS traversal over V vertices and E edges.
space_complexity_explanation: The adjacency list and visited set each use O(V + E) and O(V) space respectively.
key_insight: Removing a node and checking connectivity via BFS reveals the largest remaining connected component after maintenance.

title: Count Binary Substrings
hints: Group consecutive identical characters and record the length of each group.
For adjacent groups, the number of valid substrings is the minimum of their lengths.
Sum up min(prev, curr) for each pair of adjacent groups.
approach: Track the length of the previous and current groups of consecutive characters. For each transition, add min(prev, curr) to the answer since that many valid substrings span the boundary.
time_complexity_explanation: We make a single pass through the string.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two counters (prev and curr) are used.
key_insight: Valid binary substrings span the boundary between groups of 0s and 1s, and the count at each boundary is min(left_group_size, right_group_size).

title: Reverse Vowels of a String
hints: Use two pointers, one at the start and one at the end of the string.
Move each pointer inward until it points to a vowel.
Swap the vowels and continue until the pointers meet.
approach: Use two pointers from both ends. Advance left until a vowel is found, advance right until a vowel is found, swap them, and repeat until pointers cross.
time_complexity_explanation: Each character is visited at most once by the two pointers.
space_complexity_explanation: The string is converted to a mutable array of length n.
key_insight: Two pointers converging from both ends efficiently reverse only the vowel characters in place.

title: Longest String Chain
hints: Sort words by length so you only look at shorter predecessors.
For each word, try removing each character one at a time to form a predecessor.
Use a hash map to store the longest chain ending at each word.
approach: Sort words by length. For each word, generate all possible predecessors (remove one character at a time). Look up each predecessor in a hash map and take the max chain length + 1. Store the result for the current word.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of n words, we generate up to L predecessors (L = max word length), each taking O(L) to create via string slicing.
space_complexity_explanation: The hash map stores n words, each up to length L.
key_insight: Sort by length and for each word try all single-character deletions as predecessors, using DP to track the longest chain ending at each word.

title: Maximum Palindromes After Operations
hints: You can freely redistribute characters across all strings, so pool all characters together.
Count pairs of characters — each pair can fill two symmetric positions in a palindrome.
Sort strings by length and greedily assign pairs to shorter strings first to maximize count.
approach: Pool all characters from all strings. Count the total number of character pairs. Sort string lengths ascending. Greedily assign pairs to make each string a palindrome, starting from shortest (needs fewest pairs). A string of length L needs L//2 pairs plus at most 1 odd center.
time_complexity_explanation: O(n * L) to count all characters across all words, O(n log n) to sort the lengths.
space_complexity_explanation: O(26) for the frequency map and O(n) for the sorted lengths array.
key_insight: Since characters can be freely redistributed, pool all characters, count pairs, sort string lengths ascending, and greedily fill the shortest strings first.

title: Basic Calculator
hints: Use a stack to handle nested parentheses by saving and restoring the running result and sign.
Process digits to form numbers, apply the current sign for + and - operations.
When hitting '(', push the current result and sign onto the stack; when hitting ')', pop and combine.
approach: Maintain a running result and current sign. When encountering '(', push result and sign to stack and reset. When encountering ')', pop sign and previous result, combine. Process digits into numbers and add to result with the current sign.
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def calculate(self, s: str) -> int:
        stack = []
        result = 0
        num = 0
        sign = 1
        for ch in s:
            if ch.isdigit():
                num = num * 10 + int(ch)
            elif ch == '+':
                result += sign * num
                num = 0
                sign = 1
            elif ch == '-':
                result += sign * num
                num = 0
                sign = -1
            elif ch == '(':
                stack.append(result)
                stack.append(sign)
                result = 0
                sign = 1
            elif ch == ')':
                result += sign * num
                num = 0
                result *= stack.pop()  # sign before parenthesis
                result += stack.pop()  # result before parenthesis
        result += sign * num
        return result
time_complexity_explanation: We process each character exactly once in a single pass.
space_complexity_explanation: In the worst case with deeply nested parentheses, the stack can grow to O(n).
key_insight: Use a stack to save the outer result and sign when entering parentheses, then restore and combine when closing them.

title: Nth Highest Salary
hints: Use DENSE_RANK() or a subquery with DISTINCT to find unique salary rankings.
Handle the edge case where N is larger than the number of distinct salaries by returning NULL.
LIMIT with OFFSET can be used but remember to compute OFFSET = N - 1.
approach: Create a function that uses LIMIT 1 OFFSET N-1 on distinct salaries ordered descending. If no row exists at that offset, return NULL.
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting distinct salaries takes O(n log n) where n is the number of rows.
space_complexity_explanation: Storing distinct salaries requires O(n) space.
key_insight: Use OFFSET N-1 with LIMIT 1 on distinct sorted salaries to directly retrieve the Nth highest, returning NULL automatically if it doesn't exist.

title: Replace Employee ID With The Unique Identifier
hints: Use a LEFT JOIN to keep all employees even if they don't have a unique identifier.
Join the Employees table with the EmployeeUNI table on the id column.
Select the unique_id and name columns from the joined result.
approach: LEFT JOIN the Employees table with EmployeeUNI on employee id to get the unique_id for each employee, returning NULL for employees without one.
solution_python:
SELECT eu.unique_id, e.name
FROM Employees e
LEFT JOIN EmployeeUNI eu ON e.id = eu.id
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The JOIN operation processes n employees and m unique identifier records, typically O(n + m) with hash join.
space_complexity_explanation: The result set contains at most n rows, one per employee.
key_insight: LEFT JOIN preserves all employees while optionally attaching their unique identifier, returning NULL when no match exists.

title: Shortest Common Supersequence
hints: First compute the LCS (Longest Common Subsequence) of the two strings.
Build the supersequence by interleaving characters from both strings, using the LCS as the shared backbone.
Backtrack through the LCS DP table to construct the result.
approach: Compute the LCS DP table. Then backtrack from dp[m][n] to build the supersequence: when characters match, include once; otherwise include the character from whichever direction we move and advance that pointer. Append remaining characters from either string.
time_complexity_explanation: The LCS DP table has m * n cells, each computed in O(1) (ignoring string operations). Reconstruction is O(m + n).
space_complexity_explanation: The DP table stores strings in an m * n grid. Each string can be up to min(m, n) in length.
key_insight: The shortest common supersequence is built by interleaving both strings around their LCS: include non-LCS characters from each string between the shared LCS characters.

title: Largest Odd Number in String
hints: A number is odd if its last digit is odd.
Scan from the right to find the rightmost odd digit.
The largest odd number is the substring from the start up to and including that digit.
approach: Scan the string from right to left. The first odd digit found defines the largest odd prefix — return the substring from index 0 to that position (inclusive). If no odd digit exists, return empty string.
time_complexity_explanation: We scan at most n characters from right to left.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant amount of extra space is used (the returned substring references the original).
key_insight: The largest odd number in a numeric string is simply the longest prefix ending at the rightmost odd digit.

title: Robot Bounded In Circle
hints: Simulate the instructions once and check the robot's final position and direction.
If the robot returns to the origin, it's bounded.
If the robot does NOT face north after one cycle, it will eventually return to the origin (within 4 cycles).
approach: Simulate one cycle of instructions, tracking position and direction. The robot is bounded if it returns to origin OR it's not facing north (meaning subsequent cycles will form a closed loop).
time_complexity_explanation: We process each instruction character exactly once.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a fixed number of variables are used for position and direction.
key_insight: After one cycle, the robot is bounded if it returns to origin OR faces a different direction than north, because a non-north direction guarantees it will loop back within 4 cycles.

title: Valid Perfect Square
hints: Use binary search between 1 and num to find if any integer squared equals num.
The search space is 1 to num (or num/2 + 1 for optimization).
Compare mid*mid with num to decide which half to search.
approach: Binary search for an integer whose square equals num. If mid*mid == num, return true. If mid*mid < num, search right half; otherwise search left half.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search halves the search space each iteration, giving O(log n).
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used.
key_insight: Binary search on the integer range [1, num] to find if any integer's square equals num, without using built-in sqrt.

title: Increasing Triplet Subsequence
hints: Maintain two variables: the smallest and second smallest values seen so far.
If you find a value larger than both, you have an increasing triplet.
Update the smallest and second smallest greedily as you scan.
approach: Track two values: first (smallest so far) and second (smallest value greater than first). Iterate through the array: if a number is greater than second, return true. Otherwise update first or second appropriately.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through the array with constant work per element.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two variables are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: Greedily maintain the smallest and second-smallest values; any value exceeding both proves an increasing triplet exists.

title: Set Intersection Size At Least Two
hints: Sort intervals by their right endpoint, breaking ties by left endpoint descending.
Greedily pick the two largest values from the current interval's right end.
Track the two most recently picked values and only add new values when an interval isn't covered.
approach: Sort intervals by right endpoint ascending (ties broken by left descending). Maintain the two largest chosen values. For each interval, if neither value is in range, pick end-1 and end. If only one is in range, pick end. This greedy approach minimizes the set size.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting dominates at O(n log n). The greedy scan is O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: The result array can grow up to O(n) in the worst case.
key_insight: Sort by right endpoint and greedily assign the rightmost values possible, so they have the best chance of covering future intervals.

title: Unique Length-3 Palindromic Subsequences
hints: A length-3 palindrome has the form a_b_a where the first and last characters are the same.
For each character, find its first and last occurrence in the string.
Count the number of distinct characters between the first and last occurrence.
approach: For each of the 26 lowercase letters, find the first and last occurrence. The number of unique length-3 palindromes with that letter as the outer characters equals the number of distinct characters between those two positions.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of 26 characters, we scan the string to find first/last occurrence and count distinct characters between them.
space_complexity_explanation: The set of distinct characters between any two positions has at most 26 entries.
key_insight: A length-3 palindrome is defined by its outer character; for each letter, count distinct middle characters between its first and last occurrence.

title: Greatest Sum Divisible by Three
hints: Track the maximum sum achievable for each remainder (0, 1, 2) when divided by 3.
For each number, update all three remainder states by considering whether to include the current number.
The answer is the maximum sum with remainder 0.
approach: Use DP with three states tracking the maximum sum for each remainder mod 3. For each number, compute new states by adding the number to each existing state and updating the remainder accordingly. The answer is dp[0] (max sum with remainder 0).
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through the array once, doing constant work (3 state updates) per element.
space_complexity_explanation: Only 3 DP states are maintained regardless of input size.
key_insight: Tracking maximum sums by their remainder mod 3 lets us greedily build the largest divisible-by-three sum in one pass.

title: Department Top Three Salaries
hints: Use DENSE_RANK() to rank salaries within each department.
DENSE_RANK handles ties correctly — employees with the same salary get the same rank.
Filter for ranks <= 3 to get the top three distinct salaries per department.
approach: Use the DENSE_RANK() window function partitioned by department and ordered by salary descending to rank employees. Then filter for ranks 1, 2, and 3 to get employees whose salary is among the top three distinct salaries in their department.
solution_python:
SELECT d.name AS Department, e.name AS Employee, e.salary AS Salary
FROM (
    SELECT *, DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY departmentId ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rnk
    FROM Employee
) e
JOIN Department d ON e.departmentId = d.id
WHERE e.rnk <= 3;
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The window function requires sorting employees within each department by salary, which is O(n log n).
space_complexity_explanation: The subquery creates a temporary result set containing all employees with their rank.
key_insight: DENSE_RANK() partitioned by department handles ties naturally and identifies the top three distinct salary tiers per department.

title: Kth Smallest Element in a Sorted Matrix
hints: Binary search on the value range rather than on indices.
For a given mid value, count how many elements in the matrix are <= mid using the sorted property.
Start from the bottom-left corner to count elements <= mid in O(n) time.
approach: Binary search on the value range [matrix[0][0], matrix[n-1][n-1]]. For each mid value, count elements <= mid by starting from the bottom-left corner and moving right or up. Narrow the search until we find the smallest value with at least k elements <= it.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search runs O(log(max-min)) iterations, each counting elements in O(n) time using the staircase approach.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a few variables are used for binary search bounds and counting.
key_insight: Binary searching on value range and counting elements <= mid exploits both row and column sorting to avoid materializing all elements.

title: Minimum Increments to Equalize Leaf Paths
hints: Work bottom-up: at each node, you want all subtree paths to have equal cost.
At each internal node, increment the shorter child path to match the longer one.
The total increments needed is the sum of differences between sibling path lengths at every node.
approach: This is LeetCode 2673: Make Costs of Paths Equal in a Binary Tree. Given a perfect binary tree as an array, use a bottom-up greedy approach. At each internal node, the shorter child path is incremented to match the longer one. Process nodes from leaves to root, accumulating the cost differences.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through all internal nodes once, performing constant work at each.
space_complexity_explanation: We modify the cost array in place and use only a constant number of extra variables.
key_insight: Greedily equalizing sibling paths bottom-up ensures each fix is locally optimal and globally minimizes total increments.

title: Smallest Integer Divisible by K
hints: The number consisting of n ones can be computed as previous * 10 + 1, but you only need to track its remainder mod k.
If k is divisible by 2 or 5, no repunit will ever be divisible by k.
By the pigeonhole principle, if a solution exists, it will be found within k iterations.
approach: Iteratively compute remainders of repunit numbers (1, 11, 111, ...) modulo k. Each step: remainder = (remainder * 10 + 1) % k. If remainder becomes 0, return the length. If k is even or divisible by 5, return -1 immediately. By pigeonhole, if no solution in k steps, return -1.
time_complexity_explanation: At most k iterations are needed before a remainder repeats (pigeonhole principle).
space_complexity_explanation: Only the current remainder and loop counter are stored.
key_insight: Track only the remainder mod k instead of the actual repunit number, and use the pigeonhole principle to bound iterations.

title: Paths in Matrix Whose Sum Is Divisible by K
hints: Use a 3D DP where the third dimension tracks the current path sum modulo k.
At each cell, combine results from the cell above and the cell to the left.
The answer is dp[m-1][n-1][0] — paths reaching bottom-right with sum divisible by k.
approach: Use DP where dp[i][j][r] counts paths from (0,0) to (i,j) with path sum having remainder r mod k. For each cell, add the cell's value to remainders from top and left neighbors. The answer is dp[m-1][n-1][0].
time_complexity_explanation: We visit each cell and for each cell iterate through k remainder states, doing constant work per state.
space_complexity_explanation: The 3D DP table stores k remainder counts for each of the m*n cells.
key_insight: Adding a remainder dimension to the standard grid path DP lets us count only paths whose total sum is divisible by k.

title: Palindrome Pairs
hints: For each word, check if any prefix is a palindrome — then the reverse of the suffix could be prepended.
Similarly, if any suffix is a palindrome, the reverse of the prefix could be appended.
Use a hash map of reversed words for O(1) lookup of potential pair partners.
approach: Build a map from each reversed word to its index. For each word, check all prefix/suffix splits. If a prefix is a palindrome and the reversed suffix exists in the map, we can form a palindrome pair. Similarly for palindromic suffixes. Handle the empty string and full-word cases carefully.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of n words of average length k, we check k+1 splits, each involving O(k) palindrome check and O(k) hash lookup.
space_complexity_explanation: The hash map stores n reversed words, each of average length k.
key_insight: Splitting each word at every position and checking if one part is a palindrome while looking up the reverse of the other in a hash map covers all possible palindrome pairs.

title: Minimum Cost to Cut a Stick
hints: Sort the cuts and add 0 and n as boundaries to define all possible segments.
Use interval DP: dp[i][j] = minimum cost to make all cuts between boundary i and boundary j.
The cost of a cut is the length of the current segment (boundaries[j] - boundaries[i]).
approach: Sort cuts and prepend 0 / append n as boundaries. Use interval DP where dp[i][j] represents the minimum cost to process all cuts between boundaries[i] and boundaries[j]. For each interval, try every cut point and take the minimum cost plus the segment length.
time_complexity_explanation: Where c is the number of cuts. Three nested loops over the cuts array for interval DP.
space_complexity_explanation: The 2D DP table has dimensions (c+2) x (c+2).
key_insight: Treating this as interval DP over sorted cut positions (with stick endpoints as boundaries) lets us optimally decide the order of cuts.

title: Decrypt String from Alphabet to Integer Mapping
hints: Process the string from right to left or look ahead for '#' to determine if the current group is 1 or 2 digits.
If a '#' follows two digits, those two digits map to letters j-z (10#-26#).
Single digits 1-9 map directly to letters a-i.
approach: Iterate through the string. If the character two positions ahead is '#', take the two-digit number and map it to a letter (10->j, ..., 26->z). Otherwise, take the single digit and map it to a letter (1->a, ..., 9->i).
time_complexity_explanation: We scan through the string once, processing each character at most once.
space_complexity_explanation: The output string can be up to n characters long.
key_insight: Look ahead two positions for '#' to decide whether to consume one digit or two digits for the current character mapping.

title: Find Words Containing Character
hints: Simply iterate through each word and check if it contains the target character.
Use the 'in' operator (Python) or includes() (JS) for a clean membership check.
Collect and return the indices of all matching words.
approach: Iterate through the words array. For each word, check if it contains the given character. If it does, add the index to the result list. Return the result.
time_complexity_explanation: We check each of n words, and each word check scans up to m characters where m is the average word length.
space_complexity_explanation: The result array can contain up to n indices in the worst case.
key_insight: A straightforward linear scan checking character membership in each word is optimal for this problem.

title: Most Stones Removed with Same Row or Column
hints: Two stones sharing the same row or column are connected — model this as a graph.
The answer is total stones minus the number of connected components.
Use Union-Find on rows and columns: unify row i with column j (offset to avoid collision) for each stone.
approach: Use Union-Find where each unique row and column is a node. For each stone at (r, c), union row r with column c (offset columns by a large number to avoid collision). The answer is total stones minus the number of connected components.
time_complexity_explanation: Union-Find operations are nearly O(1) amortized with path compression, applied n times for n stones.
space_complexity_explanation: The parent map stores entries for each unique row and column, which is at most 2n.
key_insight: Using bitwise NOT (~c) for columns creates distinct keys from rows, letting Union-Find on rows and columns count connected components elegantly.

title: Count of Smaller Numbers After Self
hints: Modified merge sort can count inversions — here we count how many elements from the right half are smaller.
During the merge step, when an element from the right is placed before elements from the left, increment their counts.
Track original indices alongside values to map counts back to the correct positions.
approach: Use a modified merge sort that counts, for each element, how many smaller elements from its right side get merged before it. Pair each number with its original index to track counts correctly through the sort.
time_complexity_explanation: Merge sort divides the array log n times, and each level processes all n elements in linear time.
space_complexity_explanation: Temporary arrays for merging and the index tracking array each use O(n) space.
key_insight: Modified merge sort naturally counts how many smaller elements from the right cross over each element during merging, giving the count of smaller numbers after self.

title: Count Mentions Per User
hints: Sort events by timestamp. If timestamps tie, process OFFLINE events before MESSAGE events.
Track each user's online status and when they come back online.
Handle 'ALL' and 'HERE' mentions specially — ALL mentions all users, HERE mentions only currently online users.
approach: Sort events by timestamp (offline before message on ties). Track each user's back-online time. For each message event, if it mentions 'ALL', increment all users. If 'HERE', increment only online users. Otherwise, increment the specifically mentioned users.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting events takes O(e log e), then processing each event may touch all n users for ALL/HERE mentions.
space_complexity_explanation: Arrays for mentions and online times are O(n) where n is the number of users.
key_insight: Sorting events with offline-before-message tie-breaking ensures user online status is correctly updated before processing messages at the same timestamp.

title: Number of Smooth Descent Periods of a Stock
hints: A smooth descent period is a contiguous subarray where each element is exactly 1 less than the previous.
Track the length of the current smooth descent streak ending at each position.
Each position contributes (current streak length) new smooth descent periods.
approach: Iterate through prices. Maintain the current streak length of consecutive descent (each price is exactly 1 less than the previous). Each position contributes its streak length to the total count (a streak of length k adds k new subarrays ending there).
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the prices array with constant work per element.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two variables (streak and result) are maintained.
key_insight: A streak of length k ending at position i contributes exactly k new smooth descent subarrays, so accumulating streak lengths gives the total count.

title: Detonate the Maximum Bombs
hints: Build a directed graph: bomb A has an edge to bomb B if B is within A's blast radius.
The relationship is directional — A can trigger B doesn't mean B can trigger A.
Run BFS/DFS from each bomb and track the maximum number of bombs reachable.
approach: Build a directed graph where an edge from bomb i to bomb j exists if the distance between their centers is <= radius of bomb i. Then BFS/DFS from each bomb to find the maximum number of reachable bombs.
time_complexity_explanation: Building the graph is O(n^2), and running BFS from each of n nodes traverses up to O(n^2) edges total, giving O(n^3).
space_complexity_explanation: The adjacency list can store up to O(n^2) edges in the worst case.
key_insight: Modeling bomb chain reactions as a directed graph (A triggers B doesn't imply B triggers A) and finding the max reachable set via BFS from each start.

title: Longest Nice Substring
hints: A nice substring contains both uppercase and lowercase of every letter in it.
If any character doesn't have its counterpart, the answer cannot span across that character.
Split at the first bad character and recursively check both halves.
approach: Use divide and conquer. For the current substring, find the first character whose counterpart (upper/lower) is missing. Split at that position and recursively solve both halves. Return the longer result. Base case: if all characters have counterparts, the whole string is nice.
time_complexity_explanation: In the worst case, each recursive call reduces the string by 1 character and creates substrings, leading to O(n^2) work.
space_complexity_explanation: Recursive calls create substring copies; the maximum recursion depth and total substring space is O(n^2).
key_insight: A character without its case counterpart can never be part of a nice substring, so it's a natural split point for divide and conquer.

title: Can Make Arithmetic Progression From Sequence
hints: An arithmetic progression has a constant difference between consecutive terms.
Sort the array first, then check if all consecutive differences are equal.
The common difference should be (max - min) / (n - 1).
approach: Sort the array. Then verify that the difference between every pair of consecutive elements is the same. If all differences are equal, the sequence can form an arithmetic progression.
time_complexity_explanation: Dominated by the sorting step. The subsequent linear scan is O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: Sorting is done in place (or O(log n) for the sort stack), and only a constant number of variables are used.
key_insight: After sorting, an arithmetic progression has a uniform difference between all consecutive elements, which is trivial to verify.

title: Guess Number Higher or Lower
hints: This is a classic binary search problem on the range [1, n].
Use the guess API to determine if your pick is too high, too low, or correct.
Narrow the search range by half each time based on the API response.
approach: Binary search on [1, n]. At each step, guess the midpoint. If guess() returns -1, the target is lower (search left half). If 1, search right half. If 0, we found it.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search halves the range each iteration, converging in at most log2(n) steps.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two pointers (lo, hi) and a midpoint variable are used.
key_insight: The guess API provides the comparison oracle needed for binary search, making this a direct application of binary search on a value range.

title: GCD of Odd and Even Sums
hints: Separate elements into those at even indices (0, 2, 4, ...) and odd indices (1, 3, 5, ...).
Compute the sum of each group, then find their GCD.
Use the Euclidean algorithm for an efficient GCD computation.
approach: Compute the sum of elements at even indices and the sum of elements at odd indices. Return the GCD of these two sums using the Euclidean algorithm.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass to compute both sums, then O(log(min(a,b))) for GCD which is dominated by the linear scan.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two sum variables and GCD temporaries are used.
key_insight: Partitioning by index parity and applying the Euclidean GCD algorithm on the two sums is straightforward and efficient.

title: Remove All Adjacent Duplicates in String II
hints: Use a stack that stores pairs of (character, count).
For each character, if it matches the top of the stack, increment the count; otherwise push a new pair.
When a count reaches k, pop that entry — this handles cascading removals automatically.
approach: Use a stack of (character, count) pairs. For each character in the string, if it matches the stack top, increment the count. If the count reaches k, pop the pair. Otherwise, push a new pair. Reconstruct the result from the stack.
time_complexity_explanation: Each character is pushed and popped at most once, giving linear total work.
space_complexity_explanation: The stack can hold up to n entries in the worst case when no duplicates are removed.
key_insight: A stack of (char, count) pairs naturally handles cascading removals — when k adjacent duplicates form after a previous removal, the stack count immediately reflects it.

title: Maximize Happiness of Selected Children
hints: Sort the happiness values in descending order to pick the happiest children first.
Each subsequent pick reduces the happiness of remaining children by 1, so subtract the turn index.
Stop picking when the adjusted happiness becomes zero or negative.
approach: Sort happiness values in descending order. Greedily select children, subtracting the turn number from each child's happiness (since all remaining values decrease by 1 per selection). Accumulate only positive adjusted values.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting the array dominates at O(n log n). The greedy selection loop is O(k) which is at most O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: Sorting is done in-place, so only constant extra space is used.
key_insight: By sorting descending and greedily picking, each child picked at turn i has their happiness reduced by i, so we maximize total by always picking the currently happiest child.

title: Binary Searchable Numbers in an Unsorted Array
hints: A number is binary searchable if all numbers to its left are smaller and all numbers to its right are larger.
Precompute prefix maximums and suffix minimums.
Compare each element against the prefix max and suffix min to determine if it is binary searchable.
approach: Compute prefix maximum (max of all elements to the left) and suffix minimum (min of all elements to the right). An element is binary searchable if it is greater than the prefix max and less than the suffix min.
time_complexity_explanation: Three linear passes through the array: one for prefix max, one for suffix min, and one to count valid elements.
space_complexity_explanation: Two auxiliary arrays of size n are used for prefix maximums and suffix minimums.
key_insight: An element can be found by binary search regardless of pivot choices if and only if everything to its left is smaller and everything to its right is larger.

title: Squares of a Sorted Array
hints: The largest squared values come from either the leftmost (most negative) or rightmost (most positive) elements.
Use two pointers starting from both ends and fill the result array from the back.
Compare absolute values at both pointers to decide which square goes next.
approach: Use two pointers at the start and end of the array. Compare absolute values and place the larger square at the end of the result array, moving inward. This produces a sorted result in O(n) time.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through the array using two pointers, each element visited exactly once.
space_complexity_explanation: The result array of size n is required for the output.
key_insight: In a sorted array with negatives, the largest squares are at the extremes. Two pointers from both ends let us build the sorted squared array in one pass.

title: Minimize Maximum of Array
hints: The answer is the maximum of the ceiling of prefix averages.
You can redistribute values leftward but not rightward, so the prefix sum divided by count gives a lower bound.
Binary search on the answer also works: check if a given max is achievable by simulating redistribution.
approach: The minimum possible maximum equals the maximum ceiling of prefix averages. For each prefix of length i+1, compute ceil(prefixSum / (i+1)). The answer is the max over all these values, since we can only shift excess leftward.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the array computing running prefix sums and ceilings.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: Since we can only move value from right to left (decrement right, increment left), the bottleneck is the prefix average at each position — we can never make the max lower than ceil(prefixSum[0..i] / (i+1)).

title: Design a Stack With Increment Operation
hints: Use an array-based stack with a separate increment array for lazy propagation.
When incrementing bottom k elements, only mark the k-th element in the increment array.
On pop, propagate the increment value down to the element below for O(1) operations.
approach: Maintain a stack array and an increment array. The increment operation lazily stores the value at index min(k, size)-1. On pop, add the increment at the top to the element and propagate it to the element below, achieving O(1) for all operations.
time_complexity_explanation: All three operations (push, pop, increment) run in constant time thanks to lazy propagation of increments.
space_complexity_explanation: O(n) space for the stack and the auxiliary increment array where n is maxSize.
key_insight: Lazy propagation: instead of incrementing all bottom k elements, store the increment at position k-1 and propagate downward only when popping.

title: House Robber IV
hints: Binary search on the answer: the minimum capability (max value among robbed houses).
For a given capability, greedily check if you can rob at least k non-adjacent houses with all values <= capability.
Scan left to right; rob a house if its value is within capability and skip the next house.
approach: Binary search on the capability (the max value stolen). For each candidate, greedily count how many non-adjacent houses can be robbed with values <= candidate. If count >= k, the capability is feasible.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search over the value range m (max - min) with O(n) greedy check per iteration gives O(n log m).
space_complexity_explanation: Only constant extra space is used for pointers and counters.
key_insight: Binary search on the answer: the minimum capability is monotonic — if you can rob k houses with capability c, you can also do it with any capability > c.

title: Minimum Operations to Make Binary Array Elements Equal to One I
hints: Process the array left to right; when you find a 0, flip the window of size 3 starting at that position.
If a 0 is found within the last 2 positions and cannot start a full window, return -1.
Each flip toggles three consecutive elements; greedy left-to-right is optimal.
approach: Greedily scan left to right. When a 0 is encountered, flip it and the next two elements. If a 0 is found where a full flip window of 3 cannot fit, return -1. Count the total flips.
time_complexity_explanation: Single left-to-right pass through the array. Each element is visited once (flips are constant work).
space_complexity_explanation: Modifications are done in-place with no extra data structures.
key_insight: A greedy left-to-right approach works because flipping at the earliest 0 is always optimal — delaying a flip never helps and can only leave trailing zeros.

title: Find Winner on a Tic Tac Toe Game
hints: Track counts for each row, column, and both diagonals for each player.
After each move, check if any line reaches a count of 3.
If all 9 moves are made with no winner, it is a draw; otherwise it is pending.
approach: Simulate the game by tracking row, column, and diagonal counts. Player A uses odd moves, B uses even. After each move, check if the current player completed a row, column, or diagonal of 3.
time_complexity_explanation: Iterate through the moves list once, where m is the number of moves (at most 9).
space_complexity_explanation: Fixed-size arrays for a 3x3 board — constant space regardless of input.
key_insight: Instead of checking the entire board after each move, track cumulative counts per row, column, and diagonal to detect a winner in O(1) per move.

title: Four Divisors
hints: A number has exactly four divisors if it is p^3 (cube of a prime) or p*q (product of two distinct primes).
Iterate divisors up to sqrt(n) to count them efficiently.
Stop early if the divisor count exceeds 4.
approach: For each number, enumerate its divisors by checking up to sqrt(n). Count divisors and sum them. If exactly 4 divisors are found, add the sum to the result.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of n numbers, we check divisors up to sqrt(m) where m is the value. Early termination keeps it fast.
space_complexity_explanation: The divisor set holds at most 5 elements before we break, so it is effectively constant space.
key_insight: Enumerate divisors up to sqrt(n) and early-exit if more than 4 are found, making the check efficient for each number.

title: Project Employees I
hints: Join the Project and Employee tables on employee_id.
Group by project_id to get per-project aggregations.
Use AVG() to compute the average experience years, rounded to 2 decimal places.
approach: Join the Project table with the Employee table on employee_id, then group by project_id and compute the average experience_years using the AVG aggregate function, rounding to 2 decimals.
solution_python:
SELECT p.project_id, ROUND(AVG(e.experience_years), 2) AS average_years
FROM Project p
JOIN Employee e ON p.employee_id = e.employee_id
GROUP BY p.project_id;
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The query performs a join and group-by aggregation, both linear in the number of rows.
space_complexity_explanation: Space is proportional to the number of distinct projects in the output.
key_insight: A simple JOIN + GROUP BY + AVG pattern solves this — the key is joining on employee_id and grouping by project_id.

title: Valid Triangle Number
hints: Sort the array first so you can use the triangle inequality efficiently.
Fix the largest side and use two pointers to find valid pairs for the other two sides.
For sides a <= b <= c, only check a + b > c since the other inequalities are automatically satisfied.
approach: Sort the array. Fix the largest side c at index k from right to left. Use two pointers i=0 and j=k-1 to find pairs where nums[i] + nums[j] > nums[k]. If valid, all indices from i to j-1 also form valid triangles with j and k.
time_complexity_explanation: After O(n log n) sorting, the two-pointer scan for each fixed side is O(n), done n times giving O(n^2) total.
space_complexity_explanation: Sorting is in-place and only constant extra variables are used.
key_insight: After sorting, fixing the largest side and using two pointers on the remaining elements lets us count all valid triangles in O(n^2) using the property that if a+b > c, then all values between a and b also work.

title: Maximum Level Sum of a Binary Tree
hints: Use BFS (level-order traversal) to compute the sum of each level.
Track the level with the maximum sum.
Return the smallest level number in case of ties.
approach: Perform BFS level by level. Compute the sum of node values at each level. Track the maximum sum and its corresponding level number (1-indexed). Return the level with the highest sum.
time_complexity_explanation: Each node is visited exactly once during the BFS traversal.
space_complexity_explanation: The queue can hold up to O(n) nodes in the worst case (widest level of the tree).
key_insight: BFS naturally processes one level at a time, making it straightforward to compute per-level sums and find the maximum.

title: Find Minimum Time to Finish All Jobs
hints: Binary search on the answer: the maximum working time any worker takes.
For a given time limit, use backtracking to assign jobs to workers, pruning when a worker exceeds the limit.
Sort jobs in descending order to prune more aggressively.
approach: Binary search on the maximum working time. For each candidate, backtrack to assign jobs to k workers such that no worker exceeds the limit. Sort jobs descending for better pruning. Skip duplicate worker loads to avoid redundant states.
time_complexity_explanation: Worst case each of n jobs can go to any of k workers, but pruning (sorting, duplicate skipping, bound checking) makes it practical.
space_complexity_explanation: O(n) recursion depth plus O(k) for the workers array.
key_insight: Aggressive pruning is essential: sort descending to hit bounds early, skip workers with duplicate current loads, and prune when adding a job would exceed the current best answer.

title: Shortest Distance from All Buildings
hints: Run a BFS from each building to compute distances to all reachable empty cells.
Track how many buildings can reach each empty cell; only consider cells reachable by all buildings.
The answer is the minimum total distance among cells reachable by every building.
approach: For each building, run BFS to compute the distance to every reachable empty cell. Accumulate distances and reachability counts. The answer is the cell with minimum total distance that is reachable from all buildings.
time_complexity_explanation: BFS from each of B buildings traverses the entire m*n grid, giving O(B * m * n).
space_complexity_explanation: Visited arrays, distance arrays, and reach count arrays are all O(m * n).
key_insight: Run BFS from each building (not from each empty cell) to efficiently compute accumulated distances, then check which empty cell is reachable from all buildings with minimum total distance.

title: Largest Component Size by Common Factor
hints: Two numbers sharing a common factor > 1 should be in the same component.
Use Union-Find: for each number, union it with all its prime factors.
Map each number to a prime factor representative, then count the largest connected component.
approach: Use Union-Find. For each number, find its prime factors and union the number with each prime factor. After processing all numbers, count the size of each component by grouping numbers by their root representative.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of n numbers, factorization takes O(sqrt(m)) where m is the max value. Union-Find operations are nearly O(1) amortized.
space_complexity_explanation: The parent map stores entries for all numbers and their prime factors.
key_insight: Union each number with its prime factors rather than checking all pairs. This efficiently connects numbers sharing any common factor via their shared primes.

title: Binary Tree Cameras
hints: Use a bottom-up greedy approach: place cameras as high as possible (on parents rather than leaves).
Each node can be in one of three states: needs coverage, has a camera, or is covered.
Process leaves first — do not put cameras on leaves; instead place them on leaf parents.
approach: DFS post-order traversal with three states: 0 = needs coverage, 1 = has camera, 2 = covered. Leaves return 0 (need coverage). If any child needs coverage, place a camera on the current node. If any child has a camera, current is covered. Otherwise, current needs coverage.
time_complexity_explanation: Each node is visited exactly once in the DFS traversal.
space_complexity_explanation: Recursion stack depth equals the height h of the tree, O(n) worst case for skewed trees.
key_insight: Greedy bottom-up: never place cameras on leaves. Instead, force cameras onto their parents, which covers three generations (parent, children, grandparent) — maximizing coverage per camera.

title: Bitwise ORs of Subarrays
hints: Maintain a set of all possible OR values ending at the current position.
For each new element, OR it with every value in the previous set and add the element itself.
The number of distinct OR values in the set is bounded by the number of bits (at most 30).
approach: For each position, maintain the set of all distinct OR values of subarrays ending at that position. OR is monotonically increasing (only sets bits), so the set size is bounded by ~30. Collect all values across all positions.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of n elements, the current set has at most 30 entries (one per bit position), so OR operations per step are bounded by 30.
space_complexity_explanation: The result set can hold at most O(n * 30) distinct values across all positions.
key_insight: OR only sets bits, never clears them. So the set of distinct OR values ending at any position has at most 30 elements (one per bit), making the approach efficient despite seeming quadratic.

title: Minimum Depth of Binary Tree
hints: BFS finds the minimum depth most efficiently since it stops at the first leaf level.
Be careful with nodes that have only one child — they are not leaves.
DFS works too but must explore the entire tree.
approach: Use BFS level-order traversal. The first time we encounter a leaf node (no children), return the current depth. This guarantees the minimum depth without exploring the full tree.
time_complexity_explanation: In the worst case (balanced tree), BFS visits all nodes. In best case, it stops early at the shallowest leaf.
space_complexity_explanation: The BFS queue can hold up to O(n) nodes at the widest level.
key_insight: BFS is ideal for minimum depth because it naturally explores level by level and can return immediately upon finding the first leaf, unlike DFS which must explore all paths.

title: Keys and Rooms
hints: Model rooms as a directed graph where keys are edges.
Start DFS/BFS from room 0 and visit all reachable rooms.
Check if the number of visited rooms equals the total number of rooms.
approach: Use DFS or BFS starting from room 0. Each room contains keys to other rooms (edges). Track visited rooms and check if all rooms are reachable from room 0.
time_complexity_explanation: Visit each room once (n rooms) and process each key once (k total keys across all rooms).
space_complexity_explanation: The visited set and stack together use O(n) space in the worst case.
key_insight: This is simply a graph reachability problem — rooms are nodes, keys are directed edges. DFS/BFS from room 0 checks if the graph is fully connected from the start.

title: Sliding Window Median
hints: Maintain two heaps (max-heap for lower half, min-heap for upper half) like the median finder.
Use lazy deletion to handle removing elements that leave the window.
Rebalance heaps after each insertion and deletion to maintain the median invariant.
approach: Use a sorted list (or two heaps with lazy deletion). For each window position, insert the new element and remove the outgoing element from the sorted structure. The median is the middle element (or average of two middle elements).
time_complexity_explanation: For each of n elements, insertion and deletion from the sorted structure take O(log k) time.
space_complexity_explanation: The sorted window maintains exactly k elements at any time.
key_insight: Maintaining a sorted structure of the current window lets you find the median in O(1) and update in O(log k). Python's SortedList makes this clean; in JS, binary search with splice achieves O(k) per operation.

title: Shortest Bridge
hints: Find one island using DFS and mark all its cells.
Add all boundary cells of the first island to a BFS queue.
Expand BFS level by level until you reach the second island; the level count is the answer.
approach: Use DFS to find and mark the first island, then BFS from its boundary to find the shortest path to the second island.
time_complexity_explanation: We visit each cell at most once during DFS and BFS across the n x n grid.
space_complexity_explanation: The visited array and queue can hold up to n^2 cells.
key_insight: Treat the first island as a multi-source BFS starting point; the BFS level when reaching the second island equals the minimum flips.

title: Longest Ideal Subsequence
hints: Track the longest subsequence ending with each character.
For each character, check all characters within distance k in the alphabet.
Use a dp array of size 26 indexed by character.
approach: Use a dp array of size 26 where dp[c] is the longest ideal subsequence ending with character c. For each character, update dp[c] by checking all characters within distance k.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of n characters we check at most 2k+1 entries in the dp array, but k <= 25 so it is effectively O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: We only use a fixed-size array of 26 elements.
key_insight: Instead of tracking subsequences per index, track the best subsequence length ending at each alphabet character and update in a sliding window of size 2k+1.

title: Sliding Puzzle
hints: Represent the board as a string and use BFS over states.
The neighbors of position i in a flattened 2x3 board are predefined.
Use a set to track visited states and return the BFS level when reaching the goal.
approach: Flatten the 2x3 board into a string. Use BFS from the initial state, swapping the zero with adjacent positions, until reaching '123450'.
time_complexity_explanation: There are at most (m*n)! board permutations and each state takes O(m*n) to process.
space_complexity_explanation: We store up to (m*n)! states in the visited set.
key_insight: Treat each board configuration as a graph node and use BFS to find the shortest path to the solved state.

title: Critical Connections in a Network
hints: An edge is a bridge if removing it disconnects the graph.
Use Tarjan's bridge-finding algorithm with discovery and low-link values.
An edge (u, v) is a bridge if low[v] > disc[u].
approach: Apply Tarjan's algorithm: perform DFS tracking discovery time and lowest reachable ancestor. An edge (u,v) is critical if low[v] > disc[u].
time_complexity_explanation: Tarjan's algorithm visits every vertex and edge exactly once.
space_complexity_explanation: We store the adjacency list, discovery/low arrays, and recursion stack.
key_insight: A bridge edge (u,v) exists when no node in v's subtree can reach u or an ancestor of u via a back edge, detected when low[v] > disc[u].

title: Car Pooling
hints: Think of each trip as adding passengers at the start and removing at the end.
Use a difference array or sweep line approach.
Check if the running sum ever exceeds capacity.
approach: Use a difference array: for each trip, add passengers at the pickup and subtract at the drop-off. Scan the array and check if capacity is ever exceeded.
time_complexity_explanation: We process n trips and scan the difference array of size m (max location).
space_complexity_explanation: The difference array uses O(m) space where m is the max stop location.
key_insight: Model passenger changes as a difference array so a single prefix sum pass reveals the peak occupancy at every point.

title: 3Sum Closest
hints: Sort the array first to enable the two-pointer technique.
Fix one element and use two pointers for the remaining pair.
Track the closest sum and update when a better one is found.
approach: Sort the array, fix one element, and use two pointers to find the pair that brings the three-sum closest to the target.
time_complexity_explanation: We fix one element and use a two-pointer scan for each, giving O(n^2) total.
space_complexity_explanation: Sorting uses O(log n) space; no additional data structures are needed.
key_insight: Sorting enables the two-pointer technique: if the sum is too small move the left pointer right, if too large move the right pointer left.

title: Defanging an IP Address
hints: Replace every '.' with '[.]'.
Use a built-in string replace method.
This is a simple string manipulation problem.
approach: Replace every occurrence of '.' in the IP address string with '[.]'.
time_complexity_explanation: We scan through the string once to perform the replacement.
space_complexity_explanation: We create a new string of similar length.
key_insight: A straightforward string replacement where each dot becomes '[.]'.

title: Count Residue Prefixes
hints: Compute running prefix sums modulo a given value.
Use a hash map to count how many times each residue has appeared.
Return the count of the target residue.
approach: Iterate through the array, maintaining a running prefix sum modulo k. Use a hash map to count occurrences of each residue.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through the array once, performing constant-time operations per element.
space_complexity_explanation: The hash map stores at most k distinct residues.
key_insight: Two prefix sums with the same remainder mod k define a subarray whose sum is divisible by k.

title: Richest Customer Wealth
hints: Sum each row to get each customer's total wealth.
Return the maximum row sum.
A simple iteration problem.
approach: Sum each customer's bank accounts and return the maximum total.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through all m customers and their n bank accounts.
space_complexity_explanation: We only track the running maximum, using constant extra space.
key_insight: The richest customer is simply the one with the largest row sum in the matrix.

title: Find the Largest Area of Square Inside Two Rectangles
hints: The intersection of two axis-aligned rectangles is also a rectangle.
Find the overlap rectangle for each pair and check if a square fits.
The largest square side is min(overlap width, overlap height).
approach: For every pair of rectangles, compute their intersection. The largest inscribed square has side length equal to min(overlap_width, overlap_height). Return the maximum area.
time_complexity_explanation: We check every pair of rectangles, giving O(n^2) pairs.
space_complexity_explanation: Only constant extra variables are used.
key_insight: The intersection of two axis-aligned rectangles is itself a rectangle; the largest inscribed square has side equal to the minimum of the intersection's width and height.

title: Build an Array With Stack Operations
hints: Simulate reading numbers 1 to n from a stream.
Push every number; if it's not in target, immediately pop it.
Use a pointer to track your position in the target array.
approach: Iterate from 1 to n. Push each number. If it does not match the current target element, also pop it. If it matches, advance the target pointer.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through numbers 1 to n at most once.
space_complexity_explanation: The result array can contain up to 2n operations.
key_insight: Numbers not in the target must be pushed then immediately popped; numbers in the target are just pushed.

title: How Many Numbers Are Smaller Than the Current Number
hints: Count the frequency of each number.
Build a prefix sum of the counts.
For each number, the prefix sum tells how many are smaller.
approach: Count frequencies, compute prefix sums, then look up the count of smaller elements for each number in O(1).
time_complexity_explanation: We count frequencies in O(n) and build prefix sums in O(k) where k is the value range (101).
space_complexity_explanation: The count and prefix arrays use O(k) space.
key_insight: Counting sort gives the rank of each element without needing to sort: prefix[v] = number of elements less than v.

title: Remove Duplicate Letters
hints: Use a stack to build the result greedily.
Pop from the stack if the current character is smaller and the top character appears later.
Track which characters are already in the stack with a set.
approach: Use a monotonic stack: for each character, pop larger characters from the stack if they appear later in the string, ensuring the smallest lexicographic order.
time_complexity_explanation: Each character is pushed and popped from the stack at most once.
space_complexity_explanation: The stack and set hold at most 26 characters (constant).
key_insight: Greedily pop larger characters from the stack when a smaller one arrives, but only if the larger character appears again later.

title: Next Greater Element III
hints: This is the next permutation problem on the digits of n.
Find the rightmost digit smaller than the digit to its right.
Swap it with the smallest larger digit to its right, then reverse the suffix.
approach: Apply the next permutation algorithm on the digits of n. If no next permutation exists or the result exceeds 32-bit int, return -1.
time_complexity_explanation: We process the digits of n, where d is the number of digits (at most 10).
space_complexity_explanation: We store the digits array of length d.
key_insight: Finding the next greater number with the same digits is exactly the next permutation problem applied to the digit array.

title: Calculator with Method Chaining
hints: Store the running result in the constructor.
Each method modifies the result and returns 'this' for chaining.
The getResult method returns the current value.
approach: Create a Calculator class that stores a result value. Each arithmetic method updates the result and returns 'this' to enable chaining. getResult returns the final value.
time_complexity_explanation: Each operation performs a single arithmetic computation.
space_complexity_explanation: Only one numeric value is stored.
key_insight: Returning 'this' from each method enables fluent method chaining, a common JavaScript pattern.

title: Super Ugly Number
hints: Maintain a pointer for each prime factor.
At each step, choose the minimum candidate from all primes.
Advance all pointers whose product equals the minimum to avoid duplicates.
approach: Use the ugly number generation pattern with multiple pointers, one per prime. At each step, pick the minimum candidate and advance all pointers that produced it.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of n ugly numbers, we check k prime candidates.
space_complexity_explanation: We store n ugly numbers and k pointers.
key_insight: Each prime maintains a pointer into the dp array; the next ugly number is the minimum product across all primes with their current pointed value.

title: Construct the Minimum Bitwise Array II
hints: For ans[i] OR (ans[i] + 1) = nums[i], think about the bit pattern of nums[i].
Find the lowest 0-bit in nums[i]; flipping the bit below it gives the answer.
If nums[i] is all 1s in binary, no valid answer exists.
approach: For each nums[i], find the position of the lowest unset bit. The answer is nums[i] with the bit just below that position cleared. If nums[i] is all 1s, return -1.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of n numbers, we scan up to log M bits where M is the max value.
space_complexity_explanation: We store the result array of size n.
key_insight: If x OR (x+1) = v, then x equals v with the bit just below the lowest 0-bit of v turned off.

title: Reverse String II
hints: Process the string in chunks of 2k characters.
Reverse the first k characters in each chunk.
Handle the last chunk carefully if fewer than k characters remain.
approach: Iterate in steps of 2k, reversing the first k characters in each step.
time_complexity_explanation: We process each character at most once across all chunks.
space_complexity_explanation: We convert the string to an array of characters.
key_insight: Process the string in fixed-size chunks of 2k, only reversing the first half of each chunk.

title: Find Beautiful Indices in the Given Array I
hints: Find all indices where pattern a occurs and where pattern b occurs.
For each occurrence of a, check if any occurrence of b is within distance k.
Use two pointers or binary search for efficient pairing.
approach: Find all start indices of patterns a and b in s. For each index of a, use two pointers to check if any index of b is within distance k.
time_complexity_explanation: Finding all occurrences takes O(n * pattern_length) with naive search; the two-pointer pass is O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: We store the occurrence indices which can be up to O(n).
key_insight: After collecting sorted occurrence lists for both patterns, a two-pointer scan efficiently checks the proximity constraint.

title: Rank Scores
hints: Use the DENSE_RANK() window function to assign ranks.
Order by score descending to rank highest first.
DENSE_RANK avoids gaps in ranking when there are ties.
approach: Use the DENSE_RANK() window function ordered by score descending to assign consecutive ranks without gaps.
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The database sorts n rows to compute the window function ranking.
space_complexity_explanation: The result set contains n rows with the score and rank columns.
key_insight: DENSE_RANK() assigns consecutive ranks without gaps, unlike RANK() which skips ranks after ties.

title: Sum of Two Integers
hints: Use XOR to add without carrying.
Use AND shifted left to compute the carry.
Repeat until there is no carry left.
approach: Use bit manipulation to simulate addition. XOR gives the sum without carry, AND shifted left gives the carry. Repeat until carry is zero. In Python, handle negative numbers with a 32-bit mask.
time_complexity_explanation: At most 32 iterations for 32-bit integers, so constant time.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a few integer variables are used.
key_insight: XOR performs addition without carry, and AND with left shift computes the carry. Iterating these two operations simulates full addition.

title: Minimum Pair Removal to Sort Array I
hints: Simulate the process: find an adjacent pair whose sum replaces them.
After each removal, check if the array is sorted.
Since the array is small, brute force simulation works.
approach: Simulate the process. In each step, find the leftmost adjacent pair that is not non-decreasing, replace them with their sum, and repeat until the array is sorted. Count the number of operations.
time_complexity_explanation: Each operation reduces the array size by 1, and each scan is O(n), giving O(n^2) worst case.
space_complexity_explanation: Operations are done in-place on the input array.
key_insight: Greedily merge the adjacent pair with the smallest sum each step until the array is sorted.

title: Basic Calculator III
hints: Handle operator precedence: * and / before + and -.
Use a stack or recursion to handle parentheses.
When encountering '(', recurse or push context; on ')', return result.
approach: Use a recursive descent parser or a stack-based approach. Maintain a stack for addition/subtraction. Process multiplication/division immediately. For parentheses, recursively evaluate the sub-expression.
time_complexity_explanation: Each character is processed exactly once during the parse.
space_complexity_explanation: The stack and recursion depth can be proportional to the length of the expression.
key_insight: Recursive descent naturally handles nested parentheses while a stack manages operator precedence between +/- and */÷.

title: Contains Duplicate II
hints: Use a hash map to store the last index of each element.
Check if the current element was seen within the last k indices.
Alternatively, maintain a sliding window set of size k.
approach: Use a hash map to store the most recent index of each number. For each element, check if it exists in the map and the difference between indices is at most k.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the array with O(1) hash map lookups.
space_complexity_explanation: The hash map can store up to n entries in the worst case.
key_insight: A hash map tracking the last seen index of each value lets you check the distance constraint in O(1) per element.

title: Minimum Pair Removal to Sort Array II
hints: The brute force from part I is too slow for large arrays.
Use a sorted set or heap to efficiently find the minimum-sum pair.
Track unsorted adjacent pairs to know when the array becomes sorted.
approach: Use a doubly linked list for efficient removal, a min-heap (or sorted set) keyed by pair sum to find the minimum pair quickly, and a counter of unsorted adjacent pairs. On each merge, update neighbors in the heap and the unsorted count.
time_complexity_explanation: Each element is merged at most once, and each heap operation is O(log n).
space_complexity_explanation: The linked list, heap, and auxiliary arrays all use O(n) space.
key_insight: Use a min-heap for pair sums and a linked list for efficient neighbor tracking, counting unsorted pairs to detect when the array is sorted.

title: Invalid Tweets
hints: Use the LENGTH or CHAR_LENGTH function to check tweet content length.
Filter tweets where the content exceeds 15 characters.
Select the tweet_id of invalid tweets.
approach: Select tweet IDs where the character length of the content column is strictly greater than 15.
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The query scans all rows in the Tweets table once.
space_complexity_explanation: No additional storage beyond the result set.
key_insight: CHAR_LENGTH counts characters (not bytes), which is the correct measure for tweet length validation.

title: Maximum Number of Events That Can Be Attended
hints: Sort events by start day.
Use a min-heap to track the earliest-ending events available each day.
Greedily attend the event that ends soonest.
approach: Sort events by start day. Iterate through each day, pushing all events starting on that day into a min-heap keyed by end day. Pop expired events. Attend the event ending soonest.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting is O(n log n) and each event is pushed/popped from the heap once at O(log n) each.
space_complexity_explanation: The heap can hold up to n events.
key_insight: Greedy: each day, attend the event that ends soonest among all available events to maximize total attendance.

title: Two Sum IV - Input is a BST
hints: Traverse the BST and use a set to track seen values.
For each node, check if (k - node.val) exists in the set.
Alternatively, do an in-order traversal and apply two pointers.
approach: Traverse the BST (BFS or DFS). For each node, check if (k - node.val) exists in a hash set. If yes, return true. Otherwise, add node.val to the set.
time_complexity_explanation: Each node is visited once during the traversal.
